Step 1 — Data Preparation¶

In [259]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.dates as mdates
import os
import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

for folder in ['tables', 'plots', 'data']:
    os.makedirs(folder, exist_ok=True)

plt.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 120
plt.rcParams['font.size'] = 11

DATA_FILE = 'data_hec_projet_1.xlsx'

Load raw data¶

metadata fills the first 5 rows, so we skip them.

In [260]:
daily_raw = pd.read_excel(DATA_FILE, sheet_name='Daily', header=5, index_col=0, parse_dates=True)
monthly_raw = pd.read_excel(DATA_FILE, sheet_name='Monthly', header=5, index_col=0, parse_dates=True)

print('Daily:', daily_raw.shape, '|', daily_raw.index[0].date(), 'to', daily_raw.index[-1].date())
print('Monthly:', monthly_raw.shape, '|', monthly_raw.index[0].date(), 'to', monthly_raw.index[-1].date())
print()
print('Daily columns:', list(daily_raw.columns))
print('Monthly columns:', list(monthly_raw.columns))

Daily: (9443, 13) | 1990-01-01 to 2026-03-11
Monthly: (435, 8) | 1989-12-31 to 2026-02-28

Daily columns: ['PX_LAST', 'PX_LAST.1', 'PX_LAST.2', 'PX_LAST.3', 'PX_LAST.4', 'PX_LAST.5', 'PX_LAST.6', 'PX_LAST.7', 'PX_LAST.8', 'PX_LAST.9', 'PX_LAST.10', 'PX_LAST.11', 'PX_LAST.12']
Monthly columns: ['PX_LAST', 'PX_LAST.1', 'PX_LAST.2', 'PX_LAST.3', 'PX_LAST.4', 'PX_LAST.5', 'PX_LAST.6', 'PX_LAST.7']
In [261]:
# column positions from the output
daily_cols = {
    daily_raw.columns[1]: 'brent',
    daily_raw.columns[6]: 'msci_world',
    daily_raw.columns[7]: 'msci_em',
    daily_raw.columns[9]: 'us10y',
}
daily = daily_raw[list(daily_cols.keys())].rename(columns=daily_cols).copy()

monthly_cols = {
    monthly_raw.columns[0]: 'ip',
    monthly_raw.columns[1]: 'cfnai',
    monthly_raw.columns[2]: 'ism',
}
monthly = monthly_raw[list(monthly_cols.keys())].rename(columns=monthly_cols).copy()

daily.head(3)
Out[261]:
brent msci_world msci_em us10y
Dates
1990-01-01 19.69 567.34 214.69 7.935
1990-01-02 19.90 568.96 217.30 7.930
1990-01-03 20.95 569.10 220.45 7.974

Clean data and compute returns¶

In [262]:
# Dataset treats missing values as the string '#N/A N/A'
daily = daily.replace('#N/A N/A', np.nan).apply(pd.to_numeric, errors='coerce')
monthly = monthly.replace('#N/A N/A', np.nan).apply(pd.to_numeric, errors='coerce')

daily = daily.dropna(how='all')

print('After cleaning:')
print('Daily:', daily.shape[0], 'obs,', daily.index[0].date(), 'to', daily.index[-1].date())
print('Monthly:', monthly.shape[0], 'obs')
print()
print(daily.isna().sum())

After cleaning:
Daily: 9443 obs, 1990-01-01 to 2026-03-11
Monthly: 435 obs

brent         0
msci_world    0
msci_em       0
us10y         0
dtype: int64
In [263]:
# log-returns: r_t = ln(P_t / P_{t-1})
daily['roil'] = np.log(daily['brent'] / daily['brent'].shift(1))
daily['rworld'] = np.log(daily['msci_world'] / daily['msci_world'].shift(1))
daily['rem'] = np.log(daily['msci_em'] / daily['msci_em'].shift(1))

# yield changes (first difference, not log for rates)
daily['rrate'] = daily['us10y'].diff()

# asymmetric oil decomposition: Oil+ and Oil-
daily['oil_pos'] = np.where(daily['roil'] > 0, daily['roil'], 0)
daily['oil_neg'] = np.where(daily['roil'] < 0, daily['roil'], 0)

df_daily = daily[['roil', 'rworld', 'rem', 'rrate', 'oil_pos', 'oil_neg']].dropna()

print(df_daily.shape)
df_daily.head()

(9442, 6)
Out[263]:
roil rworld rem rrate oil_pos oil_neg
Dates
1990-01-02 0.010609 0.002851 0.012084 -0.005 0.010609 0.000000
1990-01-03 0.051419 0.000246 0.014392 0.044 0.051419 0.000000
1990-01-04 -0.008148 0.003368 0.030600 -0.002 0.000000 -0.008148
1990-01-05 -0.001445 -0.008194 0.007058 0.012 0.000000 -0.001445
1990-01-08 -0.052435 0.002821 -0.008864 0.028 0.000000 -0.052435

Build monthly dataset¶

Monthly oil return = sum of daily log-returns in the month (equivalent to ln(P_end / P_start)). For equities we take end-of-month prices and compute log-returns from those.

In [264]:
roil_monthly = daily['roil'].resample('ME').sum().rename('roil_monthly')

# end-of-month prices, then monthly log-returns
world_eom = daily['msci_world'].resample('ME').last()
em_eom = daily['msci_em'].resample('ME').last()
us10y_eom = daily['us10y'].resample('ME').last()

rworld_m = np.log(world_eom / world_eom.shift(1)).rename('rworld_monthly')
rem_m = np.log(em_eom / em_eom.shift(1)).rename('rem_monthly')
rrate_m = us10y_eom.diff().rename('rrate_monthly')

# align macro index to end of month before merging
monthly.index = monthly.index + pd.offsets.MonthEnd(0)

df_monthly = pd.concat([roil_monthly, rworld_m, rem_m, rrate_m, monthly], axis=1)
df_monthly = df_monthly.dropna(subset=['roil_monthly'])

# recession dummy: CFNAI below -0.7 is typically associated with recessionary conditions
df_monthly['recession'] = (df_monthly['cfnai'] < -0.7).astype(int)

df_monthly['oil_pos_m'] = np.where(df_monthly['roil_monthly'] > 0, df_monthly['roil_monthly'], 0)
df_monthly['oil_neg_m'] = np.where(df_monthly['roil_monthly'] < 0, df_monthly['roil_monthly'], 0)

print(df_monthly.shape)
print('Recession months (CFNAI < -0.7):', df_monthly['recession'].sum())
df_monthly.head()

(435, 10)
Recession months (CFNAI < -0.7): 35
Out[264]:
roil_monthly rworld_monthly rem_monthly rrate_monthly ip cfnai ism recession oil_pos_m oil_neg_m
Dates
1990-01-31 -0.006113 NaN NaN NaN 61.7290 -0.23 47.2 0 0.0 -0.006113
1990-02-28 -0.005123 -0.045564 0.037652 0.097 62.2896 0.55 49.1 0 0.0 -0.005123
1990-03-31 -0.046251 -0.064181 -0.149526 0.113 62.5999 0.17 49.9 0 0.0 -0.046251
1990-04-30 -0.059852 -0.016463 0.062736 0.394 62.4359 -0.71 50.0 1 0.0 -0.059852
1990-05-31 -0.072835 0.098339 0.077344 -0.423 62.6258 0.02 49.5 0 0.0 -0.072835

Quick checks¶

In [265]:
print('Missing - daily:')
print(df_daily.isna().sum())
print()
print('Missing - monthly:')
print(df_monthly.isna().sum())
print()

# any extreme oil moves?
extreme = df_daily[np.abs(df_daily['roil']) > 0.20]
print(f'Days with |roil| > 20%: {len(extreme)}')
print(extreme[['roil']].sort_values('roil'))

Save¶

In [266]:
df_daily.to_csv('data/df_daily.csv')
df_monthly.to_csv('data/df_monthly.csv')
daily[['brent', 'msci_world', 'msci_em', 'us10y']].loc['1990':].to_csv('data/prices_daily.csv')

print('df_daily:', df_daily.shape)
print('df_monthly:', df_monthly.shape)

df_daily: (9442, 6)
df_monthly: (435, 10)

Overview plots¶

In [267]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 1, figsize=(13, 12), sharex=True)

vars_daily = [
    ('roil', 'Brent Oil Log-Returns', 'steelblue'),
    ('rworld', 'MSCI World Log-Returns', 'darkgreen'),
    ('rem', 'MSCI EM Log-Returns', 'darkorange'),
    ('rrate', 'US 10Y Yield Changes (pp)', 'crimson'),
]

for ax, (col, label, color) in zip(axes, vars_daily):
    ax.plot(df_daily.index, df_daily[col], color=color, linewidth=0.5, alpha=0.8)
    ax.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.6, linestyle='--')
    ax.set_ylabel(label, fontsize=9)
    ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.YearLocator(5))
    ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%Y'))

axes[0].set_title('Daily Financial Variables', fontsize=13)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('plots/daily_returns_overview.png', bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
In [268]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(13, 9), sharex=True)

ax = axes[0]
ax.plot(df_monthly.index, df_monthly['cfnai'], color='navy', linewidth=1)
ax.axhline(-0.7, color='red', linewidth=1, linestyle='--', label='Recession threshold (-0.7)')
ax.fill_between(df_monthly.index, df_monthly['cfnai'], -0.7,
                where=(df_monthly['cfnai'] < -0.7), color='red', alpha=0.2)
ax.set_ylabel('CFNAI')
ax.legend(fontsize=8)

ax = axes[1]
ax.plot(df_monthly.index, df_monthly['ip'], color='teal', linewidth=1)
ax.set_ylabel('Industrial Production')

ax = axes[2]
ax.plot(df_monthly.index, df_monthly['ism'], color='purple', linewidth=1)
ax.axhline(50, color='black', linewidth=0.8, linestyle='--', label='ISM = 50')
ax.set_ylabel('Manufacturing ISM')
ax.legend(fontsize=8)

axes[0].set_title('Monthly Macro Variables', fontsize=13)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('plots/monthly_macro_overview.png', bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

Step 2 — Preliminary Analysis & Diagnostics¶

In [269]:
from scipy import stats
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import kpss
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf


warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

for folder in ['tables', 'plots']:
    os.makedirs(folder, exist_ok=True)

plt.style.use('bmh')
plt.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 120
plt.rcParams['font.size'] = 12
plt.rcParams['axes.titlesize'] = 13
plt.rcParams['axes.labelsize'] = 11

df_daily   = pd.read_csv('data/df_daily.csv',   index_col=0, parse_dates=True)
df_monthly = pd.read_csv('data/df_monthly.csv', index_col=0, parse_dates=True)

columns = ['roil', 'rworld', 'rem', 'rrate']
labels = {
    'roil':   'Oil (Brent)',
    'rworld': 'MSCI World',
    'rem':    'MSCI EM',
    'rrate':  'US 10Y Yield Change'
}

columns_monthly = ['roil_monthly', 'rworld_monthly', 'rem_monthly', 'cfnai', 'ip', 'ism']
labels_monthly = {
    'roil_monthly':   'Oil (monthly)',
    'rworld_monthly': 'MSCI World (monthly)',
    'rem_monthly':    'MSCI EM (monthly)',
    'cfnai':          'CFNAI',
    'ip':             'Industrial Production',
    'ism':            'Manufacturing ISM'
}

print('Daily:',   df_daily.shape,   '|', df_daily.index[0].date(),   'to', df_daily.index[-1].date())
print('Monthly:', df_monthly.shape, '|', df_monthly.index[0].date(), 'to', df_monthly.index[-1].date())

Daily: (9442, 6) | 1990-01-02 to 2026-03-11
Monthly: (435, 10) | 1990-01-31 to 2026-03-31

1. Descriptive Statistics¶

In [270]:
rows = []
for col in columns:
    s = df_daily[col].dropna()
    rows.append({
        'Variable':        labels[col],
        'Obs':             len(s),
        'Mean':            s.mean(),
        'Min':             s.min(),
        'Max':             s.max(),
        'Std':             s.std(),
        'Skewness':        s.skew(),
        'Kurtosis':        s.kurtosis() + 3,
        'Excess Kurtosis': s.kurtosis(),
    })

desc_daily = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index('Variable')
desc_daily.to_csv('tables/desc_stats_daily.csv', float_format='%.4f')
print('Daily returns — descriptive statistics')
print(desc_daily.round(4).to_string())

rows = []
for col in columns_monthly:
    s = df_monthly[col].dropna()
    rows.append({
        'Variable':        labels_monthly[col],
        'Obs':             len(s),
        'Mean':            s.mean(),
        'Std':             s.std(),
        'Min':             s.min(),
        'Max':             s.max(),
        'Skewness':        s.skew(),
        'Excess Kurtosis': s.kurtosis()
    })

desc_monthly = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index('Variable')
desc_monthly.to_csv('tables/desc_stats_monthly.csv', float_format='%.4f')
print('Monthly variables — descriptive statistics')
print(desc_monthly.round(4).to_string())

2. Distribution Plots¶

In [272]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 8))
axes = axes.flatten()

for i, col in enumerate(columns):
    ax = axes[i]
    s = df_daily[col].dropna()

    ax.hist(s, bins=80, density=True, alpha=0.5, edgecolor='white', linewidth=0.3, label='Empirical')

    x = np.linspace(s.min(), s.max(), 300)
    y_norm = stats.norm.pdf(x, s.mean(), s.std())
    ax.plot(x, y_norm, color='#A60628', linewidth=1.5, label='Normal fit')

    ax.set_title(labels[col])
    ax.set_xlabel('Value')
    ax.set_ylabel('Density')
    ax.legend(fontsize=9)

plt.suptitle('Return Distributions vs. Normal', fontsize=13)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('plots/distributions_daily.png', bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
In [273]:
skew_vals = [df_daily[col].dropna().skew()     for col in columns]
kurt_vals = [df_daily[col].dropna().kurtosis() for col in columns]
var_names = [labels[col]                 for col in columns]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4))

axes[0].bar(var_names, skew_vals, edgecolor='white')
axes[0].axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8, linestyle='--')
axes[0].set_title('Skewness')
axes[0].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=15)

axes[1].bar(var_names, kurt_vals, edgecolor='white')
axes[1].axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8, linestyle='--')
axes[1].set_title('Excess Kurtosis')
axes[1].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=15)

plt.suptitle('Skewness and Excess Kurtosis — Daily Returns', fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('plots/skewness_kurtosis.png', bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

3. Normality Tests (Jarque-Bera)¶

H0 is : the series is normally distributed

In [274]:
rows = []
for col in columns:
    s = df_daily[col].dropna()
    jb_stat, jb_pval = stats.jarque_bera(s)
    rows.append({
        'Variable':       labels[col],
        'JB Statistic':   jb_stat,
        'p-value':        jb_pval,
        'Reject H0 (5%)': 'Yes' if jb_pval < 0.05 else 'No'
    })

jb_df = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index('Variable')
jb_df.to_csv('tables/jarque_bera.csv', float_format='%.4f')
print(jb_df.round(4).to_string())

                     JB Statistic  p-value Reject H0 (5%)
Variable                                                 
Oil (Brent)           138480.2877      0.0            Yes
MSCI World             47739.0495      0.0            Yes
MSCI EM                22982.5567      0.0            Yes
US 10Y Yield Change     1939.5070      0.0            Yes

4. Stationarity (Augmented Dickey-Fuller)¶

H0 is unit root, non-stationary. Lag selection via AIC.

In [275]:
def run_adf(series, name):
    result = adfuller(series.dropna(), autolag='AIC')
    return {
        'Variable':         name,
        'ADF Stat':         result[0],
        'p-value':          result[1],
        'Lags':             result[2],
        '1% CV':            result[4]['1%'],
        '5% CV':            result[4]['5%'],
        '10% CV':           result[4]['10%'],
        'Stationary (5%)':  'Yes' if result[1] < 0.05 else 'No'
    }

rows = [run_adf(df_daily[col], labels[col]) for col in columns]
adf_daily = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index('Variable')
adf_daily.to_csv('tables/adf_daily.csv', float_format='%.4f')
print('ADF tests — daily returns')
print(adf_daily.round(4).to_string())

ADF tests — daily returns
                     ADF Stat  p-value  Lags  1% CV   5% CV  10% CV Stationary (5%)
Variable                                                                           
Oil (Brent)          -22.8277      0.0    15 -3.431 -2.8618 -2.5669             Yes
MSCI World           -22.8671      0.0    17 -3.431 -2.8618 -2.5669             Yes
MSCI EM              -15.6772      0.0    28 -3.431 -2.8618 -2.5669             Yes
US 10Y Yield Change  -19.0854      0.0    22 -3.431 -2.8618 -2.5669             Yes

rows = [run_adf(df_monthly[col], labels_monthly[col]) for col in columns_monthly]
adf_monthly = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index('Variable')
adf_monthly.to_csv('tables/adf_monthly.csv', float_format='%.4f')
print('ADF tests — monthly variables')
print(adf_monthly.round(4).to_string())

ADF tests — monthly variables
                       ADF Stat  p-value  Lags   1% CV   5% CV  10% CV Stationary (5%)
Variable                                                                              
Oil (monthly)          -16.8671   0.0000     0 -3.4455 -2.8682 -2.5703             Yes
MSCI World (monthly)   -20.0494   0.0000     0 -3.4455 -2.8682 -2.5703             Yes
MSCI EM (monthly)      -17.9006   0.0000     0 -3.4455 -2.8682 -2.5703             Yes
CFNAI                  -15.2059   0.0000     1 -3.4456 -2.8683 -2.5703             Yes
Industrial Production   -2.0448   0.2673     2 -3.4456 -2.8683 -2.5704              No
Manufacturing ISM       -5.6506   0.0000     9 -3.4459 -2.8684 -2.5704             Yes

4.2 Stationarity with kpss¶

In [277]:
def run_kpss(series, name, regression='c'):
    """
    KPSS test
    H0: series is stationary
    H1: series is non-stationary
    """
    result = kpss(series.dropna(), regression=regression, nlags='auto')

    return {
        'Variable': name,
        'KPSS Stat': result[0],
        'p-value': result[1],
        'Lags': result[2],
        '1% CV': result[3]['1%'],
        '5% CV': result[3]['5%'],
        '10% CV': result[3]['10%'],
        'Stationary (5%)': 'Yes' if result[1] >= 0.05 else 'No'
    }
    
rows = [run_kpss(df_daily[col], labels[col], regression='c') for col in columns]
kpss_daily = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index('Variable')
kpss_daily.to_csv('tables/kpss_daily.csv', float_format='%.4f')

print('KPSS tests – daily returns')
print(kpss_daily.round(4).to_string())

rows = [run_kpss(df_monthly[col], labels_monthly[col], regression='c') for col in columns_monthly]
kpss_monthly = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index('Variable')
kpss_monthly.to_csv('tables/kpss_monthly.csv', float_format='%.4f')

print('KPSS tests – monthly variables')
print(kpss_monthly.round(4).to_string())

KPSS tests – daily returns
                     KPSS Stat  p-value  Lags  1% CV  5% CV  10% CV Stationary (5%)
Variable                                                                           
Oil (Brent)             0.0453      0.1     9  0.739  0.463   0.347             Yes
MSCI World              0.0893      0.1    14  0.739  0.463   0.347             Yes
MSCI EM                 0.0626      0.1     4  0.739  0.463   0.347             Yes
US 10Y Yield Change     0.1710      0.1    12  0.739  0.463   0.347             Yes
KPSS tests – monthly variables
                       KPSS Stat  p-value  Lags  1% CV  5% CV  10% CV Stationary (5%)
Variable                                                                             
Oil (monthly)             0.0495     0.10     0  0.739  0.463   0.347             Yes
MSCI World (monthly)      0.0858     0.10     3  0.739  0.463   0.347             Yes
MSCI EM (monthly)         0.0419     0.10     5  0.739  0.463   0.347             Yes
CFNAI                     0.1869     0.10     0  0.739  0.463   0.347             Yes
Industrial Production     2.5963     0.01    12  0.739  0.463   0.347              No
Manufacturing ISM         0.1583     0.10    11  0.739  0.463   0.347             Yes

5. Time Dependency¶

Autocorrelation with returns; tests for serial correlation in the mean.

Autocorrelation with squared returns; tests for volatility clustering (ARCH effects).

In [278]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 2, figsize=(13, 14))

for i, col in enumerate(columns):
    s = df_daily[col].dropna()

    plot_acf(s,    lags=30, ax=axes[i, 0], alpha=0.05,
             title=f'{labels[col]} — Returns')
    plot_acf(s**2, lags=30, ax=axes[i, 1], alpha=0.05,
             title=f'{labels[col]} — Squared Returns')

    axes[i, 0].set_xlabel('Lag (days)')
    axes[i, 1].set_xlabel('Lag (days)')

plt.suptitle('ACF of Returns and Squared Returns (volatility clustering)', fontsize=13, y=1.005)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('plots/acf_returns.png', bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
In [279]:
# Ljung-Box test at lags 5, 10, 20
# H0: no autocorrelation up to lag k
lags = [5, 10, 20]
rows = []

for col in columns:
    s = df_daily[col].dropna()
    lb_ret = acorr_ljungbox(s,    lags=lags, return_df=True)
    lb_sq  = acorr_ljungbox(s**2, lags=lags, return_df=True)

    for lag in lags:
        rows.append({
            'Variable':           labels[col],
            'Lag':                lag,
            'LB (returns)':       round(lb_ret.loc[lag, 'lb_stat'],    4),
            'p-val (returns)':    round(lb_ret.loc[lag, 'lb_pvalue'],  4),
            'LB (sq. returns)':   round(lb_sq.loc[lag,  'lb_stat'],    4),
            'p-val (sq. ret.)':   round(lb_sq.loc[lag,  'lb_pvalue'],  4)
        })

lb_df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
lb_df.to_csv('tables/ljung_box.csv', index=False, float_format='%.4f')
print(lb_df.to_string(index=False))
print('\nH0: no autocorrelation up to lag k')

           Variable  Lag  LB (returns)  p-val (returns)  LB (sq. returns)  p-val (sq. ret.)

6. Cross-Correlations¶

7. Oil Asymmetric Effects¶

Oil+ = max(r_oil, 0) and Oil− = min(r_oil, 0). We check whether positive and negative oil shocks have different effects on equity returns.

In [ ]:
In [281]:
# Plot Oil+ and Oil- over time
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(13, 6), sharex=True)

axes[0].bar(df_daily.index, df_daily['oil_pos'], color='green',  width=1, alpha=0.7, label='Oil+')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Oil+ (positive shocks)')
axes[0].legend(fontsize=9)

axes[1].bar(df_daily.index, df_daily['oil_neg'], color='red',    width=1, alpha=0.7, label='Oil−')

axes[1].legend(fontsize=9)

axes[1].xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%Y'))

# Conditional mean equity return by oil direction
pos_mask = df_daily['roil'] > 0
neg_mask = df_daily['roil'] < 0

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 5))

for ax, col, label in zip(axes,
                          ['rworld', 'rem'],
                          ['MSCI World', 'MSCI EM']):
    means = [
        df_daily.loc[pos_mask, col].mean(),
        df_daily.loc[~pos_mask & ~neg_mask, col].mean(),
        df_daily.loc[neg_mask, col].mean()
    ]
    ax.bar(['Oil > 0', 'Oil = 0', 'Oil < 0'],
           means,
           color=['green', 'gray', 'red'],
           edgecolor='white')
    ax.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8, linestyle='--')
    ax.set_title(f'{label} — Conditional Mean Return')
    ax.set_ylabel('Mean Log-Return')

plt.suptitle('Equity Returns Conditional on Oil Price Direction', fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('plots/oil_asymmetric_conditional.png', bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

# Summary table
rows = []
for col, label in [('rworld', 'MSCI World'), ('rem', 'MSCI EM')]:
    mean_pos = df_daily.loc[pos_mask, col].mean()
    mean_neg = df_daily.loc[neg_mask, col].mean()
    rows.append({
        'Equity':        label,
        'N (Oil>0)':     int(pos_mask.sum()),
        'Mean (Oil>0)':  mean_pos,
        'N (Oil<0)':     int(neg_mask.sum()),
        'Mean (Oil<0)':  mean_neg,
        'Difference':    mean_pos - mean_neg
    })

df_asymetric = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index('Equity')
df_asymetric.to_csv('tables/oil_asymmetric.csv', float_format='%.6f')
print(df_asymetric.round(6))

            N (Oil>0)  Mean (Oil>0)  N (Oil<0)  Mean (Oil<0)  Difference
Equity                                                                  
MSCI World       4743      0.001664       4443     -0.001316     0.00298
MSCI EM          4743      0.001550       4443     -0.001210     0.00276

Step 3 — Baseline OLS Models¶

0. Setup¶

In [283]:
import statsmodels.api as sm

warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Output folders — following step 1 convention
TABLES_DIR = 'tables'
PLOTS_DIR  = 'plots'
os.makedirs(TABLES_DIR, exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs(PLOTS_DIR,  exist_ok=True)

plt.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 120
plt.rcParams['font.size'] = 11

print('Libraries loaded.')

Libraries loaded.

1. Load data¶

In [284]:
df_daily = pd.read_csv('data/df_daily.csv', index_col=0, parse_dates=True)
df_monthly = pd.read_csv('data/df_monthly.csv', index_col=0, parse_dates=True)

print('Daily  :', df_daily.shape,  '|', df_daily.index[0].date(), 'to', df_daily.index[-1].date())
print('Monthly:', df_monthly.shape, '|', df_monthly.index[0].date(), 'to', df_monthly.index[-1].date())
print()
print('Daily columns :', df_daily.columns.tolist())
print('Monthly columns:', df_monthly.columns.tolist())

Daily  : (9442, 6) | 1990-01-02 to 2026-03-11
Monthly: (435, 10) | 1990-01-31 to 2026-03-31

Daily columns : ['roil', 'rworld', 'rem', 'rrate', 'oil_pos', 'oil_neg']
Monthly columns: ['roil_monthly', 'rworld_monthly', 'rem_monthly', 'rrate_monthly', 'ip', 'cfnai', 'ism', 'recession', 'oil_pos_m', 'oil_neg_m']

2. OLS from scratch: core function¶

This section implements the OLS estimator and extends it to allow Newey–West (HAC) standard errors for robust inference.

The function takes y and X (without constant), adds the constant internally, and returns:

  • beta — OLS coefficient estimates
  • se — standard errors
    • classical OLS if hac=False
    • Newey-West HAC if hac=True
  • t — t-statistics computed as $\hat{\beta} / \hat{se}$
  • r2 — coefficient of determination $R^2 = 1 - SSE/SST$
  • resid — residuals $\hat{u}_t = y_t - \hat{y}_t$

The OLS coefficients are always computed from:

$\hat{\beta} = (X'X)^{-1}X'y$

When hac=False, the covariance matrix is:

$\widehat{\mathrm{Var}}(\hat{\beta}) = \hat{\sigma}^2 (X'X)^{-1}$

When hac=True, we use a Newey-West HAC covariance matrix, which keeps the OLS coefficients unchanged but corrects the inference for heteroskedasticity and weak serial dependence.

We use np.linalg.pinv (pseudo-inverse) instead of np.linalg.inv to handle near-singular cases more robustly.

In [285]:
def ols_with_const(y, X, x_names=None, hac=False, maxlags=5):
    """
Implements OLS with an intercept from scratch.

Coefficients are estimated using the standard OLS estimator.
Optionally, the covariance matrix can be adjusted using the
Newey–West HAC estimator for robust inference.

    Parameters:
    
    y       : array-like (T,)
    X       : array-like (T,) or (T, k)   (WITHOUT the constant)
    x_names : list of regressor names (optional)
    hac     : bool, if True compute Newey-West HAC standard errors
    maxlags : int, HAC truncation lag (used only if hac=True)

    Returns:
    
    dict with keys: beta, se, t, yhat, resid, r2, sigma2, vcov, columns, nobs, dof, cov_type, maxlags
    """
    y = np.asarray(y, dtype=float).reshape(-1, 1)
    X = np.asarray(X, dtype=float)
    if X.ndim == 1:
        X = X.reshape(-1, 1)

    # Drop observations where y or any X is missing / non-finite
    ok = np.isfinite(y).flatten()
    for j in range(X.shape[1]):
        ok = ok & np.isfinite(X[:, j])
    y, X = y[ok], X[ok]

    T = y.shape[0]
    Xc = np.column_stack([np.ones(T), X])   # add constant as first column
    k  = Xc.shape[1]

    # OLS formula
    XtX     = Xc.T @ Xc
    Xty     = Xc.T @ y
    XtX_inv = np.linalg.pinv(XtX)           # pseudo-inverse: handles near-singular X'X
    beta    = (XtX_inv @ Xty).flatten()

    yhat  = Xc @ beta
    resid = y.flatten() - yhat

    # Goodness of fit
    sse = float(np.dot(resid, resid))
    sst = float(np.dot(y.flatten() - y.mean(), y.flatten() - y.mean()))
    r2  = 1.0 - sse / sst if sst > 0 else np.nan

    dof = T - k #degrees of freedom
    sigma2 = sse / dof #classical residual variance


    # Standard errors and covariance matrix
    if not hac:
        vcov = sigma2 * XtX_inv
        cov_type = 'Classical OLS'
    else:
        # Newey-West "meat" matrix
        # S = sum_t e_t^2 x_t x_t' + sum_l w_l sum_t e_t e_{t-l}(x_t x_{t-l}' + x_{t-l} x_t')
        S = np.zeros((k, k))

        # Lag 0 term
        for t in range(T):
            xt = Xc[t:t+1].T   # (k,1)
            S += resid[t]**2 * (xt @ xt.T)

        # Positive lag terms
        L = min(maxlags, T - 1)
        for ell in range(1, L + 1):
            weight = 1.0 - ell / (L + 1.0)   # Bartlett kernel
            Gamma = np.zeros((k, k))

            for t in range(ell, T):
                xt = Xc[t:t+1].T
                xt_lag = Xc[t - ell:t - ell + 1].T
                Gamma += resid[t] * resid[t - ell] * (xt @ xt_lag.T)

            S += weight * (Gamma + Gamma.T)

        vcov = XtX_inv @ S @ XtX_inv
        cov_type = f'Newey-West HAC (maxlags={maxlags})'

    se = np.sqrt(np.maximum(np.diag(vcov), 0))
    tstat = beta / se
#-----------------------------------------------------
    if x_names is None:
        columns = ['const'] + [f'x{j}' for j in range(1, k)]
    else:
        columns = ['const'] + list(x_names)

    return {
        'beta'   : beta.flatten(),
        'se'     : se.flatten(),
        't'      : tstat.flatten(),
        'yhat'   : yhat.flatten(),
        'resid'  : resid.flatten(),
        'r2'     : r2,
        'sigma2' : float(sigma2),
        'vcov'   : vcov,
        'columns': columns,
        'nobs'   : T,
        'dof'    : dof,
        'cov_type': cov_type,
        'maxlags' : maxlags
    }


def summarize_ols(res, title=''):
    """Return a clean DataFrame with beta, se, t-stat, p-value and stars for significance.
    
    Uses Normal distribution for p-values when HAC covariance is used (asymptotic),
    and Student-t when classical OLS covariance is used.
    """
    from scipy.stats import t as t_dist, norm
    df = pd.DataFrame(
        {'beta': res['beta'], 'se': res['se'], 't-stat': res['t']},
        index=res['columns']
    )
    # Two-sided p-value:
    # HAC (Newey-West) → asymptotic Normal standard
    # Classical OLS    → Student-t with T-k degrees of freedom
    if res['cov_type'].startswith('Newey-West'):
        df['p-value'] = df['t-stat'].apply(lambda t: 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(t))))
    else:
        dof = res['nobs'] - len(res['columns'])
        df['p-value'] = df['t-stat'].apply(lambda t: 2 * (1 - t_dist.cdf(abs(t), df=dof)))
    # Significance stars (|t| > 1.645 → *, > 1.96 → **, > 2.576 → ***)
    def stars(t):
        at = abs(t)
        if at > 2.576: return '***'
        if at > 1.96:  return '**'
        if at > 1.645: return '*'
        return ''
    df['sig'] = df['t-stat'].apply(stars)
    if title:
        print(f'\n--- {title} ---')
        print(f'  N = {res["nobs"]}   R² = {res["r2"]:.4f}')
    return df

def save_table(df_table, filename, title=None):
    """Save a DataFrame as PNG table in tables/."""
    display_df = df_table.copy()
    for col in display_df.columns:
        display_df[col] = display_df[col].apply(
            lambda x: f'{float(x):.4f}' if isinstance(x, (int, float)) and x != '' else x
        )
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(max(7, 1.8 * len(df_table.columns)),
                                    max(2, 0.45 * (len(df_table) + 2))))
    ax.axis('off')
    tbl = ax.table(
        cellText=display_df.values,
        rowLabels=display_df.index.tolist(),
        colLabels=display_df.columns.tolist(),
        loc='center', cellLoc='center'
    )
    tbl.auto_set_font_size(False)
    tbl.set_fontsize(9)
    tbl.scale(1, 1.3)
    if title:
        ax.set_title(title, pad=12, fontsize=10)
    plt.tight_layout()
    path = os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, filename)
    fig.savefig(path, dpi=200, bbox_inches='tight')
    plt.close(fig)
    print(f'  → saved: {path}')
In [286]:
def ljungbox_resid(res, label='', lags=[5, 10, 20]):
    """
    Applies the Ljung–Box test to OLS residuals.
    Null hypothesis: residuals are not autocorrelated up to lag k.

    Used to verify whether HAC standard errors have adequately addressed
    the autocorrelation present in the residuals.
    """
    from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox
    lb = acorr_ljungbox(res['resid'], lags=lags, return_df=True)
    lb['Reject H0 (5%)'] = lb['lb_pvalue'].apply(lambda p: 'Yes' if p < 0.05 else 'No')
    if label:
        print(f'Ljung-Box residual test — {label}')
    print(lb[['lb_stat', 'lb_pvalue', 'Reject H0 (5%)']].round(4).to_string())
    print()
In [287]:
# --- Quick sanity check ---
np.random.seed(0)
_T = 200
_x = np.random.randn(_T)
_y = 1.0 + 2.0 * _x + np.random.randn(_T)
_res = ols_with_const(_y, _x, ['x'], hac=True)
print('Sanity check — true: const=1.0, beta=2.0')
display(summarize_ols(_res, 'Sanity check'))

Sanity check — true: const=1.0, beta=2.0

--- Sanity check ---
  N = 200   R² = 0.8348
beta se t-stat p-value sig
const 0.867034 0.058237 14.888082 0.0 ***
x 2.069825 0.070945 29.175114 0.0 ***

Statsmodels verification¶

We confirm that our from-scratch implementation matches statsmodels.OLS on the sanity-check data.

In [288]:
_check = sm.OLS(_y, sm.add_constant(_x)).fit()

print('From scratch  — const: {:.6f}  beta: {:.6f}'.format(_res['beta'][0], _res['beta'][1]))
print('Statsmodels   — const: {:.6f}  beta: {:.6f}'.format(_check.params[0], _check.params[1]))
print('Max difference:', np.max(np.abs(_res['beta'] - _check.params)))
print('✓ Both implementations agree.')

From scratch  — const: 0.867034  beta: 2.069825
Statsmodels   — const: 0.867034  beta: 2.069825
Max difference: 4.440892098500626e-16
✓ Both implementations agree.

3. Baseline OLS Models (Daily frequency)¶

We estimate two baseline regressions on daily log-returns:

$$r_{world,t} = \alpha + \beta_1 r_{oil,t} + \beta_2 r_{rate,t} + \varepsilon_t$$

$$r_{em,t} = \alpha + \beta_1 r_{oil,t} + \beta_2 r_{rate,t} + \varepsilon_t$$

where:

  • $r_{oil,t}$ = Brent oil log-return
  • $r_{rate,t}$ = change in US 10Y yield
  • $r_{world,t}$ = MSCI World log-return
  • $r_{em,t}$ = MSCI Emerging Markets log-return

Interpretation of $\beta_1$: a 1% increase in oil prices is associated with a $\beta_1$% change in equity returns, controlling for the interest rate.

In [289]:
# Regressors: oil return + rate change
X_base = df_daily[['roil', 'rrate']].values

# Model 1: MSCI World
y_world = df_daily['rworld'].values
res_world = ols_with_const(y_world, X_base, ['roil', 'rrate'], hac=True, maxlags=5)

# Model 2: MSCI EM
y_em = df_daily['rem'].values
res_em = ols_with_const(y_em, X_base, ['roil', 'rrate'], hac=True, maxlags=5)

# Display results
tbl_world = summarize_ols(res_world, 'Baseline — MSCI World (daily)')
display(tbl_world.round(4))
print(f'  N = {res_world["nobs"]}   R² = {res_world["r2"]:.4f}')

tbl_em = summarize_ols(res_em, 'Baseline — MSCI EM (daily)')
display(tbl_em.round(4))
print(f'  N = {res_em["nobs"]}   R² = {res_em["r2"]:.4f}')

--- Baseline — MSCI World (daily) ---
  N = 9442   R² = 0.0666
beta se t-stat p-value sig
const 0.0002 0.0001 2.1509 0.0315 **
roil 0.0765 0.0124 6.1755 0.0000 ***
rrate 0.0265 0.0032 8.1572 0.0000 *** 
  N = 9442   R² = 0.0666

--- Baseline — MSCI EM (daily) ---
  N = 9442   R² = 0.0382
beta se t-stat p-value sig
const 0.0002 0.0001 1.5213 0.1282
roil 0.0790 0.0130 6.0849 0.0000 ***
rrate 0.0189 0.0028 6.7929 0.0000 *** 
  N = 9442   R² = 0.0382
In [290]:
# Ljung-Box residual tests — Baseline models 
ljungbox_resid(res_world, 'Baseline MSCI World (daily)')
ljungbox_resid(res_em,    'Baseline MSCI EM (daily)')

In [291]:
# Combined summary table
summary_baseline = pd.DataFrame({
    'World_beta': res_world['beta'],
    'World_tstat': res_world['t'],
    'EM_beta': res_em['beta'],
    'EM_tstat': res_em['t'],
}, index=res_world['columns'])

# Add N and R2 as footer rows
footer = pd.DataFrame({
    'World_beta': [res_world['nobs'], res_world['r2']],
    'World_tstat': ['', ''],
    'EM_beta': [res_em['nobs'], res_em['r2']],
    'EM_tstat': ['', ''],
}, index=['N', 'R²'])

summary_baseline_full = pd.concat([summary_baseline, footer])

summary_baseline.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'ols_baseline_daily.csv'))
save_table(summary_baseline.round(4), 'ols_baseline_daily.png', 
           'Baseline OLS — Daily (MSCI World vs EM)')
display(summary_baseline.round(4))

  → saved: tables\ols_baseline_daily.png
World_beta World_tstat EM_beta EM_tstat
const 0.0002 2.1509 0.0002 1.5213
roil 0.0765 6.1755 0.0790 6.0849
rrate 0.0265 8.1572 0.0189 6.7929

Residual plots — baseline models¶

In [292]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(13, 7), sharex=True)

for ax, res, label, color in zip(
    axes,
    [res_world, res_em],
    ['MSCI World', 'MSCI EM'],
    ['steelblue', 'darkorange']
):
    ax.plot(df_daily.index, res['resid'], color=color, linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.7)
    ax.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8, linestyle='--')
    ax.set_ylabel(f'{label} residuals', fontsize=9)
    ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.YearLocator(5))
    ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%Y'))

axes[0].set_title('Baseline OLS residuals — Daily', fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(PLOTS_DIR, 'ols_baseline_residuals.png'), bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print('  → saved: plots/ols_baseline_residuals.png')

  → saved: plots/ols_baseline_residuals.png

4. Asymmetric Oil Model¶

Oil price increases and decreases may have asymmetric effects on equity markets — a key hypothesis in the literature (Hamilton, 2003; Kilian & Park, 2009).

We decompose oil returns into:

  • $Oil^+_t = \max(r_{oil,t}, 0)$ — positive oil shocks (price increases)
  • $Oil^-_t = \min(r_{oil,t}, 0)$ — negative oil shocks (price decreases)

The model becomes:

$$r_{world,t} = \alpha + \beta_1 Oil^+_t + \beta_2 Oil^-_t + \beta_3 r_{rate,t} + \varepsilon_t$$

Test of asymmetry: if $\beta_1 \neq \beta_2$, oil shocks are asymmetric.

This decomposition was already computed in Step 1 (oil_pos, oil_neg).

In [293]:
X_asym = df_daily[['oil_pos', 'oil_neg', 'rrate']].values

# --- Model 3: Asymmetric — MSCI World ---
res_asym_world = ols_with_const(y_world, X_asym, ['oil_pos', 'oil_neg', 'rrate'], hac=True, maxlags=5)

# --- Model 4: Asymmetric — MSCI EM ---
res_asym_em = ols_with_const(y_em, X_asym, ['oil_pos', 'oil_neg', 'rrate'], hac=True, maxlags=5)

tbl_asym_world = summarize_ols(res_asym_world, 'Asymmetric — MSCI World (daily)')
display(tbl_asym_world.round(4))
print(f'  N = {res_asym_world["nobs"]}   R² = {res_asym_world["r2"]:.4f}')

tbl_asym_em = summarize_ols(res_asym_em, 'Asymmetric — MSCI EM (daily)')
display(tbl_asym_em.round(4))
print(f'  N = {res_asym_em["nobs"]}   R² = {res_asym_em["r2"]:.4f}')

--- Asymmetric — MSCI World (daily) ---
  N = 9442   R² = 0.0677
beta se t-stat p-value sig
const 0.0005 0.0002 2.1313 0.0331 **
oil_pos 0.0555 0.0133 4.1727 0.0000 ***
oil_neg 0.0948 0.0259 3.6659 0.0002 ***
rrate 0.0264 0.0032 8.1867 0.0000 *** 
  N = 9442   R² = 0.0677

--- Asymmetric — MSCI EM (daily) ---

beta se t-stat p-value sig
const 0.0005 0.0003 1.7616 0.0781 *
oil_pos 0.0589 0.0149 3.9581 0.0001 ***
oil_neg 0.0965 0.0280 3.4473 0.0006 ***
rrate 0.0188 0.0028 6.7852 0.0000 ***

In [294]:
# Ljung-Box residual tests,  Asymmetric models
ljungbox_resid(res_asym_world, 'Asymmetric MSCI World (daily)')
ljungbox_resid(res_asym_em,    'Asymmetric MSCI EM (daily)')

Ljung-Box residual test — Asymmetric MSCI World (daily)
     lb_stat  lb_pvalue Reject H0 (5%)
5   143.0930        0.0            Yes
10  184.7098        0.0            Yes
20  212.5966        0.0            Yes

Ljung-Box residual test — Asymmetric MSCI EM (daily)
     lb_stat  lb_pvalue Reject H0 (5%)
5   412.5808        0.0            Yes
10  449.0307        0.0            Yes
20  493.6355        0.0            Yes
In [295]:
# Test of asymmetry: H0: beta(oil_pos) = beta(oil_neg)
# A simple t-test on the difference: t = (b_pos - b_neg) / se_diff
# se_diff = sqrt(Var(b_pos) + Var(b_neg) - 2*Cov(b_pos, b_neg))

def asymmetry_test(res, idx_pos=1, idx_neg=2):
    """Test H0: beta(oil_pos) = beta(oil_neg)."""
    b_pos = res['beta'][idx_pos]
    b_neg = res['beta'][idx_neg]
    var_pos = res['vcov'][idx_pos, idx_pos]
    var_neg = res['vcov'][idx_neg, idx_neg]
    cov_pn  = res['vcov'][idx_pos, idx_neg]
    diff    = b_pos - b_neg
    se_diff = np.sqrt(var_pos + var_neg - 2 * cov_pn)
    t_diff  = diff / se_diff
    return diff, se_diff, t_diff

for label, res in [('MSCI World', res_asym_world), ('MSCI EM', res_asym_em)]:
    diff, se_d, t_d = asymmetry_test(res)
    print(f'{label}: beta_pos - beta_neg = {diff:.4f}  (t = {t_d:.3f})')
    print(f'  → Asymmetry is', 'significant at 5%' if abs(t_d) > 1.96 else 'NOT significant at 5%')

MSCI World: beta_pos - beta_neg = -0.0393  (t = -1.169)
  → Asymmetry is NOT significant at 5%
MSCI EM: beta_pos - beta_neg = -0.0376  (t = -1.006)
  → Asymmetry is NOT significant at 5%
In [296]:
# Save asymmetric results table
summary_asym = pd.DataFrame({
    'World_beta': res_asym_world['beta'],
    'World_tstat': res_asym_world['t'],
    'EM_beta': res_asym_em['beta'],
    'EM_tstat': res_asym_em['t'],
}, index=res_asym_world['columns'])

summary_asym.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'ols_asymmetric_daily.csv'))
save_table(summary_asym.round(4), 'ols_asymmetric_daily.png',
           'Asymmetric OLS — Daily (oil_pos / oil_neg)')
display(summary_asym.round(4))

  → saved: tables\ols_asymmetric_daily.png
World_beta World_tstat EM_beta EM_tstat
const 0.0005 2.1313 0.0005 1.7616
oil_pos 0.0555 4.1727 0.0589 3.9581
oil_neg 0.0948 3.6659 0.0965 3.4473
rrate 0.0264 8.1867 0.0188 6.7852

5. Macro Interaction Models (Monthly frequency)¶

The central question of the project: does the oil effect differ during recessions?

We use the CFNAI (Chicago Fed National Activity Index) to identify recession periods: $$Recession_t = \mathbf{1}[CFNAI_t < -0.7]$$

This threshold is standard in the literature — CFNAI below -0.7 is associated with recessionary conditions.

The interaction model is:

$$r_{world,t} = \alpha + \beta_1 r_{oil,t} + \beta_2 Recession_t + \beta_3 (r_{oil,t} \times Recession_t) + \varepsilon_t$$

  • $\beta_1$: oil effect in normal times
  • $\beta_1 + \beta_3$: oil effect during recessions
  • $\beta_3$: the key coefficient: does the oil effect change in bad times?
In [297]:
# Drop the 1 missing observation (Jan 1990)
df_m = df_monthly.dropna(subset=['rworld_monthly', 'rem_monthly']).copy()

print(f'Monthly sample: {df_m.index[0].date()} to {df_m.index[-1].date()}')
print(f'N = {len(df_m)}, recession months = {df_m["recession"].sum()}')

# Build interaction term
df_m['oil_x_recession'] = df_m['roil_monthly'] * df_m['recession']

# Regressors for interaction model
X_inter = df_m[['roil_monthly', 'recession', 'oil_x_recession']].values

y_world_m = df_m['rworld_monthly'].values
y_em_m    = df_m['rem_monthly'].values

# Model 5: Macro interaction — MSCI World (monthly)
res_inter_world = ols_with_const(
    y_world_m, X_inter,
    ['roil_monthly', 'recession', 'oil x recession'],
    hac=True, maxlags=3
)

# Model 6: Macro interaction — MSCI EM (monthly)
res_inter_em = ols_with_const(
    y_em_m, X_inter,
    ['roil_monthly', 'recession', 'oil x recession'],
    hac=True, maxlags=3
)

tbl_inter_world = summarize_ols(res_inter_world, 'Macro interaction — MSCI World (monthly)')
display(tbl_inter_world.round(4))
print(f'  N = {res_inter_world["nobs"]}   R² = {res_inter_world["r2"]:.4f}')

tbl_inter_em = summarize_ols(res_inter_em, 'Macro interaction — MSCI EM (monthly)')
display(tbl_inter_em.round(4))
print(f'  N = {res_inter_em["nobs"]}   R² = {res_inter_em["r2"]:.4f}')

Monthly sample: 1990-02-28 to 2026-03-31
N = 434, recession months = 35

In [299]:
# Economic interpretation: total oil effect by regime 
print('=== Total oil effect by regime ===')
print()
for label, res in [('MSCI World', res_inter_world), ('MSCI EM', res_inter_em)]:
    b_oil     = res['beta'][1]   # beta on roil_monthly
    b_inter   = res['beta'][3]   # beta on oil x recession
    oil_normal = b_oil
    oil_recession = b_oil + b_inter
    print(f'{label}:')
    print(f'  Normal times  : {oil_normal:.4f}  (beta_oil = {b_oil:.4f})')
    print(f'  Recession     : {oil_recession:.4f}  (beta_oil + beta_interaction = {oil_recession:.4f})')
    print(f'  Difference    : {b_inter:.4f}  (t = {res["t"][3]:.3f})')
    print()

=== Total oil effect by regime ===

MSCI World:
  Normal times  : 0.0912  (beta_oil = 0.0912)
  Recession     : 0.1509  (beta_oil + beta_interaction = 0.1509)
  Difference    : 0.0597  (t = 0.590)

MSCI EM:
  Normal times  : 0.1386  (beta_oil = 0.1386)
  Recession     : 0.2227  (beta_oil + beta_interaction = 0.2227)
  Difference    : 0.0841  (t = 0.601)
In [300]:
# Save interaction results
summary_inter = pd.DataFrame({
    'World_beta': res_inter_world['beta'],
    'World_tstat': res_inter_world['t'],
    'EM_beta': res_inter_em['beta'],
    'EM_tstat': res_inter_em['t'],
}, index=res_inter_world['columns'])

# Add N and R2
extra = pd.DataFrame({
    'World_beta': [res_inter_world['nobs'], round(res_inter_world['r2'], 4)],
    'World_tstat': ['', ''],
    'EM_beta': [res_inter_em['nobs'], round(res_inter_em['r2'], 4)],
    'EM_tstat': ['', ''],
}, index=['N', 'R²'])

summary_inter_full = pd.concat([summary_inter, extra])
summary_inter_full.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'ols_interaction_monthly.csv'))
save_table(summary_inter_full, 'ols_interaction_monthly.png',
           'Macro Interaction OLS — Monthly (CFNAI recession dummy)')
display(summary_inter_full)

Visualisation: oil effect in recession vs normal times¶

In [301]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))

for ax, res, label in zip(axes,
                           [res_inter_world, res_inter_em],
                           ['MSCI World', 'MSCI EM']):
    b_oil   = res['beta'][1]
    b_inter = res['beta'][3]
    se_oil  = res['se'][1]
    se_int  = res['se'][3]

    regimes = ['Normal times', 'Recession']
    betas   = [b_oil, b_oil + b_inter]
    errors  = [se_oil, np.sqrt(se_oil**2 + se_int**2 +
                                2 * res['vcov'][1, 3])]

    colors = ['steelblue', 'crimson']
    bars = ax.bar(regimes, betas, color=colors, alpha=0.75, width=0.4)
    ax.errorbar(regimes, betas, yerr=[1.96 * e for e in errors],
                fmt='none', color='black', capsize=5, linewidth=1.5)
    ax.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8, linestyle='--')
    ax.set_title(f'Oil β — {label} (monthly)', fontsize=11)
    ax.set_ylabel('Coefficient on oil return')

plt.suptitle('Oil effect by macro regime (CFNAI threshold = -0.7)', fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(PLOTS_DIR, 'ols_oil_effect_by_regime.png'), bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print('  → saved: plots/ols_oil_effect_by_regime.png')

  → saved: plots/ols_oil_effect_by_regime.png

Recession periods on the sample¶

In [302]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 4))

ax.plot(df_m.index, df_m['roil_monthly'], color='steelblue', linewidth=0.8,
        label='Monthly oil return')

# Shade recession periods
in_rec = False
rec_start = None
for date, row in df_m.iterrows():
    if row['recession'] == 1 and not in_rec:
        rec_start = date
        in_rec = True
    elif row['recession'] == 0 and in_rec:
        ax.axvspan(rec_start, date, color='red', alpha=0.15)
        in_rec = False
if in_rec:
    ax.axvspan(rec_start, df_m.index[-1], color='red', alpha=0.15)

ax.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.7, linestyle='--')
ax.set_ylabel('Monthly oil log-return')
ax.set_title('Monthly oil returns with recession periods (CFNAI < -0.7) shaded', fontsize=11)
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.YearLocator(5))
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%Y'))

from matplotlib.patches import Patch
ax.legend(handles=[
    ax.lines[0],
    Patch(facecolor='red', alpha=0.3, label='Recession (CFNAI < -0.7)')
])

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(PLOTS_DIR, 'recession_periods.png'), bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print('  → saved: plots/recession_periods.png')

  → saved: plots/recession_periods.png

6. Summary of all baseline models¶

We consolidate all results in a single comparison table.

In [303]:
def make_row(label, res, coef_name):
    """Extract a specific coefficient row for the summary table."""
    cols = res['columns']
    if coef_name not in cols:
        return None
    idx = cols.index(coef_name)
    b = res['beta'][idx]
    t = res['t'][idx]
    stars = '***' if abs(t) > 2.576 else ('**' if abs(t) > 1.96 else ('*' if abs(t) > 1.645 else ''))
    return {'Model': label, 'Coef': coef_name,
            'beta': round(b, 4), 't-stat': round(t, 3), 'sig': stars,
            'N': res['nobs'], 'R2': round(res['r2'], 4)}

rows = [
    make_row('Baseline World (daily)',    res_world,       'roil'),
    make_row('Baseline EM (daily)',       res_em,          'roil'),
    make_row('Asymmetric World (daily)',  res_asym_world,  'oil_pos'),
    make_row('Asymmetric World (daily)',  res_asym_world,  'oil_neg'),
    make_row('Asymmetric EM (daily)',     res_asym_em,     'oil_pos'),
    make_row('Asymmetric EM (daily)',     res_asym_em,     'oil_neg'),
    make_row('Interaction World (monthly)', res_inter_world, 'roil_monthly'),
    make_row('Interaction World (monthly)', res_inter_world, 'oil x recession'),
    make_row('Interaction EM (monthly)',    res_inter_em,    'roil_monthly'),
    make_row('Interaction EM (monthly)',    res_inter_em,    'oil x recession'),
]

summary_all = pd.DataFrame([r for r in rows if r is not None])
summary_all.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'ols_all_models_summary.csv'), index=False)
save_table(summary_all, 'ols_all_models_summary.png', 'All baseline OLS models — key coefficients')
display(summary_all)

  → saved: tables\ols_all_models_summary.png

Step 4 — OLS Robustness Checks¶

0. Setup¶

1. Load data¶

In [304]:
# df_monthly is already loaded above (Step 1)
# Drop the single missing observation (Jan 1990 — no Dec 1989 price for EOM returns)
df_m = df_monthly.dropna(subset=['rworld_monthly', 'rem_monthly']).copy()

print(f'Monthly sample: {df_m.index[0].date()} to {df_m.index[-1].date()}')
print(f'N = {len(df_m)}')
print()
print(df_m[['roil_monthly', 'ip', 'cfnai', 'ism', 'recession']].describe().round(3))

Monthly sample: 1990-02-28 to 2026-03-31
N = 434

       roil_monthly       ip    cfnai      ism  recession
count       434.000  433.000  433.000  433.000    434.000
mean          0.004   90.449   -0.073   52.216      0.081
std           0.093   12.821    1.115    4.884      0.273
min          -0.634   60.335  -18.260   34.500      0.000
25%          -0.046   86.202   -0.290   49.000      0.000
50%           0.008   95.026    0.010   52.600      0.000
75%           0.061  100.285    0.240   55.700      0.000
max           0.366  104.100    6.300   63.800      1.000

2. OLS from scratch¶

Same function as Step 2

3. Robustness Check 1 — Industrial Production (IP)¶

We replace the CFNAI dummy with an IP dummy: $$IP\_low_t = \mathbf{1}[IP_t < \text{median}(IP)]$$

The model: $$r_{world,t} = \alpha + \beta_1 r_{oil,t} + \beta_2 IP\_low_t + \beta_3 (r_{oil,t} \times IP\_low_t) + \varepsilon_t$$

Why IP? Industrial production is a direct measure of real economic activity. Periods below the median historically coincide with economic slowdowns and may amplify the impact of oil price changes on equity markets.

Why median? Unlike CFNAI, IP has a trend (grows over time), so using a fixed threshold would be misleading. The median splits the sample into roughly equal halves.

In [305]:
# Build IP dummy: 1 when IP is below its sample median (weak activity)
ip_median = df_m['ip'].median()
df_m['ip_low'] = (df_m['ip'] < ip_median).astype(int)

print(f'IP median: {ip_median:.2f}')
print(f'IP_low months: {df_m["ip_low"].sum()} / {len(df_m)} ({df_m["ip_low"].mean()*100:.1f}%)')

# Build interaction term
df_m['oil_x_ip_low'] = df_m['roil_monthly'] * df_m['ip_low']

X_ip = df_m[['roil_monthly', 'ip_low', 'oil_x_ip_low']].values.astype(float)

# Estimate for World and EM
res_ip_world = ols_with_const(
    df_m['rworld_monthly'].values, X_ip,
    ['roil_monthly', 'ip_low', 'oil x ip_low'],
    hac=True, maxlags=3
)
res_ip_em = ols_with_const(
    df_m['rem_monthly'].values, X_ip,
    ['roil_monthly', 'ip_low', 'oil x ip_low'],
    hac=True, maxlags=3
)

display(summarize_ols(res_ip_world, 'Robustness 1 (IP) — MSCI World').round(4))
print(f'  N = {res_ip_world["nobs"]}   R² = {res_ip_world["r2"]:.4f}')

display(summarize_ols(res_ip_em, 'Robustness 1 (IP) — MSCI EM').round(4))
print(f'  N = {res_ip_em["nobs"]}   R² = {res_ip_em["r2"]:.4f}')

IP median: 95.03
IP_low months: 216 / 434 (49.8%)

--- Robustness 1 (IP) — MSCI World ---
  N = 434   R² = 0.0718
beta se t-stat p-value sig
const 0.0055 0.0022 2.4704 0.0135 **
roil_monthly 0.1678 0.0335 5.0141 0.0000 ***
ip_low -0.0018 0.0041 -0.4264 0.6698
oil x ip_low -0.1061 0.0808 -1.3129 0.1892 
  N = 434   R² = 0.0718

--- Robustness 1 (IP) — MSCI EM ---
  N = 434   R² = 0.0669
beta se t-stat p-value sig
const 0.0031 0.0033 0.9631 0.3355
roil_monthly 0.2271 0.0362 6.2672 0.0000 ***
ip_low 0.0017 0.0066 0.2595 0.7953
oil x ip_low -0.1213 0.1050 -1.1556 0.2478 
  N = 434   R² = 0.0669
In [306]:
# Ljung-Box residual tests — Robustness Check 1 (IP)
ljungbox_resid(res_ip_world, 'Robustness IP — MSCI World')
ljungbox_resid(res_ip_em,    'Robustness IP — MSCI EM')

Ljung-Box residual test — Robustness IP — MSCI World
    lb_stat  lb_pvalue Reject H0 (5%)
5    6.4337     0.2663             No
10  11.5950     0.3131             No
20  22.2321     0.3280             No

Ljung-Box residual test — Robustness IP — MSCI EM
    lb_stat  lb_pvalue Reject H0 (5%)
5   13.5925     0.0184            Yes
10  19.7306     0.0319            Yes
20  37.0185     0.0116            Yes
In [307]:
# Economic interpretation: total oil effect by IP regime
print('=== Oil effect by IP regime ===')
for label, res in [('MSCI World', res_ip_world), ('MSCI EM', res_ip_em)]:
    b_oil  = res['beta'][1]
    b_int  = res['beta'][3]
    print(f'{label}:')
    print(f'  Normal IP (above median) : {b_oil:.4f}  (t = {res["t"][1]:.3f})')
    print(f'  Low IP (below median)    : {b_oil + b_int:.4f}  (t_interaction = {res["t"][3]:.3f})')
    print()

=== Oil effect by IP regime ===
MSCI World:
  Normal IP (above median) : 0.1678  (t = 5.014)
  Low IP (below median)    : 0.0616  (t_interaction = -1.313)

MSCI EM:
  Normal IP (above median) : 0.2271  (t = 6.267)
  Low IP (below median)    : 0.1058  (t_interaction = -1.156)

4. Robustness Check 2 — Manufacturing ISM¶

We replace the CFNAI dummy with an ISM dummy: $$ISM\_contr_t = \mathbf{1}[ISM_t < 50]$$

The model: $$r_{world,t} = \alpha + \beta_1 r_{oil,t} + \beta_2 ISM\_contr_t + \beta_3 (r_{oil,t} \times ISM\_contr_t) + \varepsilon_t$$

Why ISM < 50? The ISM Manufacturing Index measures business conditions in the US manufacturing sector. A reading below 50 indicates contraction — this is the standard threshold used by practitioners and the financial press. It is a forward-looking indicator, making it particularly relevant for asset pricing.

In [308]:
# Build ISM contraction dummy: 1 when ISM < 50 (manufacturing contraction)
df_m['ism_contr'] = (df_m['ism'] < 50).astype(int)

print(f'ISM contraction months: {df_m["ism_contr"].sum()} / {len(df_m)} ({df_m["ism_contr"].mean()*100:.1f}%)')
print(f'ISM mean: {df_m["ism"].mean():.2f},  ISM min: {df_m["ism"].min():.2f}')

df_m['oil_x_ism'] = df_m['roil_monthly'] * df_m['ism_contr']
X_ism = df_m[['roil_monthly', 'ism_contr', 'oil_x_ism']].values.astype(float)

res_ism_world = ols_with_const(
    df_m['rworld_monthly'].values, X_ism,
    ['roil_monthly', 'ism_contr', 'oil x ism_contr'],
    hac=True, maxlags=3
)
res_ism_em = ols_with_const(
    df_m['rem_monthly'].values, X_ism,
    ['roil_monthly', 'ism_contr', 'oil x ism_contr'],
    hac=True, maxlags=3
)

display(summarize_ols(res_ism_world, 'Robustness 2 (ISM) — MSCI World').round(4))
print(f'  N = {res_ism_world["nobs"]}   R² = {res_ism_world["r2"]:.4f}')

display(summarize_ols(res_ism_em, 'Robustness 2 (ISM) — MSCI EM').round(4))
print(f'  N = {res_ism_em["nobs"]}   R² = {res_ism_em["r2"]:.4f}')

ISM contraction months: 135 / 434 (31.1%)
ISM mean: 52.22,  ISM min: 34.50

--- Robustness 2 (ISM) — MSCI World ---

const 0.0040 0.0022 1.8687 0.0617 *
roil_monthly 0.1454 0.0330 4.4110 0.0000 ***
ism_contr 0.0001 0.0053 0.0268 0.9786
oil x ism_contr -0.0644 0.0858 -0.7514 0.4524 
  N = 434   R² = 0.0630

--- Robustness 2 (ISM) — MSCI EM ---
  N = 434   R² = 0.0608
beta se t-stat p-value sig
const 0.0026 0.0036 0.7156 0.4742
roil_monthly 0.1934 0.0440 4.3919 0.0000 ***
ism_contr 0.0030 0.0079 0.3793 0.7044
oil x ism_contr -0.0528 0.1072 -0.4929 0.6221 
  N = 434   R² = 0.0608
In [309]:
# Ljung-Box residual tests — Robustness Check 2 (ISM)
ljungbox_resid(res_ism_world, 'Robustness ISM — MSCI World')
ljungbox_resid(res_ism_em,    'Robustness ISM — MSCI EM')

Ljung-Box residual test — Robustness ISM — MSCI World
    lb_stat  lb_pvalue Reject H0 (5%)
5    5.5659     0.3508             No
10   9.9475     0.4451             No
20  19.2820     0.5036             No

Ljung-Box residual test — Robustness ISM — MSCI EM
    lb_stat  lb_pvalue Reject H0 (5%)
5   13.8535     0.0166            Yes
10  20.5472     0.0245            Yes
20  37.3356     0.0107            Yes
In [310]:
print('=== Oil effect by ISM regime ===')
for label, res in [('MSCI World', res_ism_world), ('MSCI EM', res_ism_em)]:
    b_oil  = res['beta'][1]
    b_int  = res['beta'][3]
    print(f'{label}:')
    print(f'  ISM >= 50 (expansion)    : {b_oil:.4f}  (t = {res["t"][1]:.3f})')
    print(f'  ISM < 50  (contraction)  : {b_oil + b_int:.4f}  (t_interaction = {res["t"][3]:.3f})')
    print()

=== Oil effect by ISM regime ===
MSCI World:
  ISM >= 50 (expansion)    : 0.1454  (t = 4.411)
  ISM < 50  (contraction)  : 0.0810  (t_interaction = -0.751)

MSCI EM:
  ISM >= 50 (expansion)    : 0.1934  (t = 4.392)
  ISM < 50  (contraction)  : 0.1405  (t_interaction = -0.493)

5. Robustness Check 3 — CFNAI continuous (no dummy)¶

Instead of binarising the CFNAI into a recession dummy, we use the raw continuous CFNAI value as both a level control and in the interaction:

$$r_{world,t} = \alpha + \beta_1 r_{oil,t} + \beta_2 CFNAI_t + \beta_3 (r_{oil,t} \times CFNAI_t) + \varepsilon_t$$

Interpretation of $\beta_3$: a negative $\beta_3$ means that when economic conditions deteriorate (CFNAI falls), the oil effect becomes more negative — which is the core hypothesis of the project.

This specification avoids the arbitrary threshold of -0.7, using the full variation of the CFNAI.

In [311]:
# CFNAI continuous interaction
df_m['oil_x_cfnai'] = df_m['roil_monthly'] * df_m['cfnai']

X_cfnai = df_m[['roil_monthly', 'cfnai', 'oil_x_cfnai']].values.astype(float)

res_cfnai_world = ols_with_const(
    df_m['rworld_monthly'].values, X_cfnai,
    ['roil_monthly', 'cfnai', 'oil x cfnai'],
    hac=True, maxlags=3
)
res_cfnai_em = ols_with_const(
    df_m['rem_monthly'].values, X_cfnai,
    ['roil_monthly', 'cfnai', 'oil x cfnai'],
    hac=True, maxlags=3
)

display(summarize_ols(res_cfnai_world, 'Robustness 3 (CFNAI continuous) — MSCI World').round(4))
print(f'  N = {res_cfnai_world["nobs"]}   R² = {res_cfnai_world["r2"]:.4f}')

display(summarize_ols(res_cfnai_em, 'Robustness 3 (CFNAI continuous) — MSCI EM').round(4))
print(f'  N = {res_cfnai_em["nobs"]}   R² = {res_cfnai_em["r2"]:.4f}')

--- Robustness 3 (CFNAI continuous) — MSCI World ---

roil_monthly 0.1047 0.0430 2.4345 0.0149 **
cfnai -0.0007 0.0027 -0.2642 0.7916
oil x cfnai -0.0239 0.0113 -2.1165 0.0343 ** 
  N = 433   R² = 0.0693

--- Robustness 3 (CFNAI continuous) — MSCI EM ---
  N = 433   R² = 0.0702
beta se t-stat p-value sig
const 0.0045 0.0033 1.3527 0.1762
roil_monthly 0.1558 0.0529 2.9477 0.0032 ***
cfnai -0.0010 0.0028 -0.3781 0.7054
oil x cfnai -0.0326 0.0130 -2.5075 0.0122 ** 
  N = 433   R² = 0.0702

ljungbox_resid(res_cfnai_em,    'Robustness CFNAI continu — MSCI EM')

In [313]:
print('=== Oil effect at selected CFNAI levels ===')
print('(Total oil effect = beta_oil + beta_interaction * CFNAI)')
print()
cfnai_scenarios = {'Severe recession (CFNAI=-2)': -2.0,
                   'Mild recession (CFNAI=-0.7)': -0.7,
                   'Normal (CFNAI=0)': 0.0,
                   'Expansion (CFNAI=+1)': 1.0}

for label, res in [('MSCI World', res_cfnai_world), ('MSCI EM', res_cfnai_em)]:
    b_oil  = res['beta'][1]
    b_int  = res['beta'][3]
    print(f'{label}  (beta_oil={b_oil:.4f}, beta_interaction={b_int:.4f}):')
    for scenario, cfnai_val in cfnai_scenarios.items():
        total_effect = b_oil + b_int * cfnai_val
        print(f'  {scenario}: {total_effect:.4f}')
    print()

=== Oil effect at selected CFNAI levels ===
(Total oil effect = beta_oil + beta_interaction * CFNAI)

MSCI World  (beta_oil=0.1047, beta_interaction=-0.0239):
  Severe recession (CFNAI=-2): 0.1525
  Mild recession (CFNAI=-0.7): 0.1214
  Normal (CFNAI=0): 0.1047
  Expansion (CFNAI=+1): 0.0808

MSCI EM  (beta_oil=0.1558, beta_interaction=-0.0326):
  Severe recession (CFNAI=-2): 0.2211
  Mild recession (CFNAI=-0.7): 0.1786
  Normal (CFNAI=0): 0.1558
  Expansion (CFNAI=+1): 0.1232

6. Comparison across all specifications¶

We now compare the key interaction coefficient across all four specifications (baseline + 3 robustness checks).

In [314]:
# Recompute baseline (CFNAI dummy) directly — no dependency on Step 2
df_m['oil_x_recession'] = df_m['roil_monthly'] * df_m['recession']
X_base_ref = df_m[['roil_monthly', 'recession', 'oil_x_recession']].values.astype(float)
_res_bw = ols_with_const(df_m['rworld_monthly'].values, X_base_ref,
                          ['roil_monthly', 'recession', 'oil x recession'],
                          hac=True, maxlags=3)
_res_be = ols_with_const(df_m['rem_monthly'].values,    X_base_ref,
                          ['roil_monthly', 'recession', 'oil x recession'],
                          hac=True, maxlags=3)
b_base_world = _res_bw['beta'][3]; t_base_world = _res_bw['t'][3]
b_base_em    = _res_be['beta'][3]; t_base_em    = _res_be['t'][3]
print(f'Baseline (CFNAI dummy): World interaction β = {b_base_world:.4f} (t={t_base_world:.3f})')
print(f'Baseline (CFNAI dummy): EM    interaction β = {b_base_em:.4f} (t={t_base_em:.3f})')


# Build comparison table
specs = ['Baseline (CFNAI dummy)', 'Check 1 (IP dummy)', 'Check 2 (ISM dummy)', 'Check 3 (CFNAI continuous)']

world_betas = [b_base_world,
               res_ip_world['beta'][3],
               res_ism_world['beta'][3],
               res_cfnai_world['beta'][3]]

world_tstats = [t_base_world,
                res_ip_world['t'][3],
                res_ism_world['t'][3],
                res_cfnai_world['t'][3]]

em_betas = [b_base_em,
            res_ip_em['beta'][3],
            res_ism_em['beta'][3],
            res_cfnai_em['beta'][3]]

em_tstats = [t_base_em,
             res_ip_em['t'][3],
             res_ism_em['t'][3],
             res_cfnai_em['t'][3]]

def sig(t):
    at = abs(t)
    return '***' if at > 2.576 else ('**' if at > 1.96 else ('*' if at > 1.645 else ''))

comparison = pd.DataFrame({
    'Specification': specs,
    'World_beta': [round(b, 4) for b in world_betas],
    'World_tstat': [round(t, 3) for t in world_tstats],
    'World_sig': [sig(t) for t in world_tstats],
    'EM_beta': [round(b, 4) for b in em_betas],
    'EM_tstat': [round(t, 3) for t in em_tstats],
    'EM_sig': [sig(t) for t in em_tstats],
}).set_index('Specification')

comparison.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'robustness_interaction_comparison.csv'))
save_table(comparison, 'robustness_interaction_comparison.png',
           'Interaction coefficient — comparison across specifications')
display(comparison)

Baseline (CFNAI dummy): World interaction β = 0.0597 (t=0.590)
Baseline (CFNAI dummy): EM    interaction β = 0.0841 (t=0.601)
  → saved: tables\robustness_interaction_comparison.png
World_beta World_tstat World_sig EM_beta EM_tstat EM_sig
Specification
Baseline (CFNAI dummy) 0.0597 0.590 0.0841 0.601
Check 1 (IP dummy) -0.1061 -1.313 -0.1213 -1.156
Check 2 (ISM dummy) -0.0644 -0.751 -0.0528 -0.493
Check 3 (CFNAI continuous) -0.0239 -2.117 ** -0.0326 -2.507 **

Visual comparison¶

In [315]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))

colors = ['steelblue', 'teal', 'darkorange', 'purple']
x_pos = np.arange(len(specs))

for ax, betas, tstats, title in zip(
    axes,
    [world_betas, em_betas],
    [world_tstats, em_tstats],
    ['MSCI World', 'MSCI EM']
):
    bars = ax.bar(x_pos, betas, color=colors, alpha=0.75, width=0.5)
    ax.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8, linestyle='--')

    # Add t-stat labels on bars
    for i, (b, t) in enumerate(zip(betas, tstats)):
        label = f't={t:.2f}{sig(t)}'
        y_pos = b + 0.001 if b >= 0 else b - 0.003
        ax.text(i, y_pos, label, ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=8)

    ax.set_xticks(x_pos)
    ax.set_xticklabels(['Baseline', 'IP', 'ISM', 'CFNAI\ncont.'], fontsize=9)
    ax.set_title(f'Interaction β — {title}', fontsize=11)
    ax.set_ylabel('Coefficient on oil × macro indicator')

plt.suptitle('Robustness: interaction coefficient across specifications', fontsize=12)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(PLOTS_DIR, 'robustness_interaction_plot.png'), bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print('  → saved: plots/robustness_interaction_plot.png')

  → saved: plots/robustness_interaction_plot.png

Macro indicators over time — what each dummy captures¶

In [316]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(13, 10), sharex=True)

# Panel 1: CFNAI with threshold
ax = axes[0]
ax.plot(df_m.index, df_m['cfnai'], color='navy', linewidth=0.9)
ax.axhline(-0.7, color='red', linewidth=1, linestyle='--', label='Threshold: -0.7')
ax.fill_between(df_m.index, df_m['cfnai'], -0.7,
                where=(df_m['cfnai'] < -0.7), color='red', alpha=0.2)
ax.set_ylabel('CFNAI')
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
ax.set_title('Macro indicators used in robustness checks', fontsize=12)

# Panel 2: IP with median
ax = axes[1]
ax.plot(df_m.index, df_m['ip'], color='teal', linewidth=0.9)
ax.axhline(ip_median, color='red', linewidth=1, linestyle='--',
           label=f'Median: {ip_median:.1f}')
ax.fill_between(df_m.index, df_m['ip'], ip_median,
                where=(df_m['ip'] < ip_median), color='red', alpha=0.2)
ax.set_ylabel('Industrial Production')
ax.legend(fontsize=8)

# Panel 3: ISM with threshold 50
ax = axes[2]
ax.plot(df_m.index, df_m['ism'], color='purple', linewidth=0.9)
ax.axhline(50, color='red', linewidth=1, linestyle='--', label='Threshold: 50')
ax.fill_between(df_m.index, df_m['ism'], 50,
                where=(df_m['ism'] < 50), color='red', alpha=0.2)
ax.set_ylabel('Manufacturing ISM')
ax.legend(fontsize=8)

for ax in axes:
    ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.YearLocator(5))
    ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%Y'))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(PLOTS_DIR, 'robustness_macro_indicators.png'), bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print('  → saved: plots/robustness_macro_indicators.png')

  → saved: plots/robustness_macro_indicators.png

7. Save all robustness tables¶

In [317]:
for tag, res_w, res_e in [
    ('ip',    res_ip_world,    res_ip_em),
    ('ism',   res_ism_world,   res_ism_em),
    ('cfnai_cont', res_cfnai_world, res_cfnai_em),
]:
    tbl = pd.DataFrame({
        'World_beta':  res_w['beta'],
        'World_tstat': res_w['t'],
        'EM_beta':     res_e['beta'],
        'EM_tstat':    res_e['t'],
    }, index=res_w['columns'])
    extra = pd.DataFrame({
        'World_beta': [res_w['nobs'], round(res_w['r2'],4)],
        'World_tstat': ['',''],
        'EM_beta': [res_e['nobs'], round(res_e['r2'],4)],
        'EM_tstat': ['','']
    }, index=['N','R²'])
    tbl_full = pd.concat([tbl, extra])
    tbl_full.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, f'robustness_{tag}.csv'))
    save_table(tbl_full, f'robustness_{tag}.png',
               f'Robustness — {tag.upper()} specification')

print('\nAll robustness tables saved.')

  → saved: tables\robustness_ip.png
  → saved: tables\robustness_ism.png
  → saved: tables\robustness_cfnai_cont.png

All robustness tables saved.

8. Files saved to tables/ and plots/¶

File Content
robustness_ip.csv/png Check 1: IP dummy
robustness_ism.csv/png Check 2: ISM dummy
robustness_cfnai_cont.csv/png Check 3: CFNAI continuous
robustness_interaction_comparison.csv/png Side-by-side comparison of interaction coefficient
robustness_interaction_plot.png Bar chart comparison
robustness_macro_indicators.png CFNAI / IP / ISM time series with thresholds

Step 5 — VAR Models & Forecasting¶

0. Setup¶

In [318]:
from statsmodels.tsa.api import VAR
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox

warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

TABLES_DIR = 'tables'
PLOTS_DIR  = 'plots'
os.makedirs(TABLES_DIR, exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs(PLOTS_DIR,  exist_ok=True)

plt.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 120
plt.rcParams['font.size'] = 11

# Utility: save a DataFrame as PNG table
def save_table(df_table, filename, title=None):
    """Save a DataFrame as PNG table in tables/ — following step 1 convention."""
    display_df = df_table.copy()
    for col in display_df.columns:
        display_df[col] = display_df[col].apply(
            lambda x: str(int(float(x))) if (isinstance(x, (int, float)) and x != '' and float(x) == int(float(x)) and abs(float(x)) < 1e10)
                      else (f'{float(x):.4f}' if isinstance(x, (int, float)) and x != '' else x)
        )
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(max(7, 1.8 * len(df_table.columns)),
                                    max(2, 0.45 * (len(df_table) + 2))))
    ax.axis('off')
    tbl = ax.table(
        cellText=display_df.values,
        rowLabels=display_df.index.tolist(),
        colLabels=display_df.columns.tolist(),
        loc='center', cellLoc='center'
    )
    tbl.auto_set_font_size(False)
    tbl.set_fontsize(9)
    tbl.scale(1, 1.3)
    if title:
        ax.set_title(title, pad=12, fontsize=10)
    plt.tight_layout()
    path = os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, filename)
    fig.savefig(path, dpi=200, bbox_inches='tight')
    plt.close(fig)
    print(f'  → saved: {path}')

1. Load data¶

Section 5 — VAR: Daily Financial Variables¶

5.1 Variable vectors¶

We estimate two daily VAR systems as specified in the project plan:

$$y_1 = \begin{pmatrix} Oil_t \\ FedRate_t \\ MSCI\ World_t \end{pmatrix} \qquad y_2 = \begin{pmatrix} Oil_t \\ FedRate_t \\ MSCI\ EM_t \end{pmatrix}$$

Oil is ordered first so that its contemporaneous shock can affect the other variables in the same period. This is the standard Cholesky identification assumption for oil price shocks.

In [319]:
# y1: Oil, Fed Rate (yield changes), MSCI World
var1_data = df_daily[['roil', 'rrate', 'rworld']].dropna()
var1_data.columns = ['Oil', 'Fed Rate', 'MSCI World']

# y2: Oil, Fed Rate, MSCI EM
var2_data = df_daily[['roil', 'rrate', 'rem']].dropna()
var2_data.columns = ['Oil', 'Fed Rate', 'MSCI EM']

print('VAR y1 (World):', var1_data.shape, '|', var1_data.index[0].date(), 'to', var1_data.index[-1].date())
print('VAR y2 (EM):   ', var2_data.shape)
print()

# Summary statistics
stats = var1_data.describe().T[['mean','std','min','max']].round(4)
display(stats)
stats.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'var_daily_summary_stats.csv'))

VAR y1 (World): (9442, 3) | 1990-01-02 to 2026-03-11
VAR y2 (EM):    (9442, 3)
mean std min max
Oil 0.0002 0.0219 -0.3856 0.1908
Fed Rate -0.0004 0.0583 -0.4736 0.3602
MSCI World 0.0002 0.0094 -0.1044 0.0910

5.2 Lag selection¶

We test lag lengths from 1 to 10 days using AIC, BIC, FPE and HQIC information criteria.

  • AIC tends to select more lags (lower penalty)
  • BIC is more parsimonious (higher penalty for additional parameters)
In [321]:
print('=== Lag selection: y1 (World) ===')
model1 = VAR(var1_data)
lag_sel1 = model1.select_order(10)

lag_table1 = pd.DataFrame({
    'AIC':  lag_sel1.ics['aic'],
    'BIC':  lag_sel1.ics['bic'],
    'HQIC': lag_sel1.ics['hqic'],
    'FPE':  lag_sel1.ics['fpe'],
})
lag_table1.index.name = 'Lag'

lag_display = lag_table1.copy()
lag_display['FPE'] = lag_table1['FPE'].apply(lambda x: f'{x:.3e}')

print(lag_sel1.summary())
display(lag_display)
lag_table1.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'var1_lag_selection.csv'))
save_table(lag_display, 'var1_lag_selection.png', 'Lag selection — y1 (World)')

=== Lag selection: y1 (World) ===
 VAR Order Selection (* highlights the minimums)  
==================================================
       AIC         BIC         FPE         HQIC   
--------------------------------------------------
0       -22.75      -22.75   1.315e-10      -22.75
1       -22.77     -22.76*   1.292e-10     -22.77*
2       -22.77      -22.75   1.291e-10      -22.77
3       -22.77      -22.75   1.289e-10      -22.76
4       -22.77      -22.74   1.290e-10      -22.76
5       -22.77      -22.73   1.290e-10      -22.76
6       -22.77      -22.73   1.287e-10      -22.76
7      -22.77*      -22.72  1.286e-10*      -22.76
8       -22.77      -22.72   1.286e-10      -22.75
9       -22.77      -22.71   1.286e-10      -22.75
10      -22.77      -22.70   1.287e-10      -22.75
--------------------------------------------------
AIC BIC HQIC FPE
Lag
0 -22.752011 -22.749736 -22.751239 1.315e-10

2 -22.770791 -22.754868 -22.765386 1.291e-10

8 -22.774144 -22.717275 -22.754838 1.286e-10
9 -22.773960 -22.710267 -22.752337 1.286e-10
10 -22.773588 -22.703070 -22.749649 1.287e-10 
  → saved: tables\var1_lag_selection.png
In [322]:
print('=== Lag selection: y2 (EM) ===')
model2 = VAR(var2_data)
lag_sel2 = model2.select_order(10)

lag_table2 = pd.DataFrame({
    'AIC':  lag_sel2.ics['aic'],
    'BIC':  lag_sel2.ics['bic'],
    'HQIC': lag_sel2.ics['hqic'],
    'FPE':  lag_sel2.ics['fpe'],
})
lag_table2.index.name = 'Lag'

lag_display2 = lag_table2.copy()
lag_display2['FPE'] = lag_table2['FPE'].apply(lambda x: f'{x:.3e}')

print(lag_sel2.summary())
display(lag_display2)
lag_table2.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'var2_lag_selection.csv'))
save_table(lag_display2, 'var2_lag_selection.png', 'Lag selection — y2 (EM)')

5.3 Estimation¶

We estimate each VAR using the lag length selected above. Each equation is estimated by OLS (reduced-form VAR).

In [226]:
p1 = max(1, lag_sel1.bic)
p2 = max(1, lag_sel2.bic)
print(f'Selected lags: y1 (World) = {p1}, y2 (EM) = {p2}')

var1_fit = model1.fit(p1)
var2_fit = model2.fit(p2)

print(var1_fit.summary())
print(var2_fit.summary())

Selected lags: y1 (World) = 1, y2 (EM) = 1

5.4 Granger causality tests¶

Granger causality tests whether past values of oil returns provide statistically significant information to predict current equity returns, beyond what is already in the equity returns' own past.

  • H0: Oil does NOT Granger-cause equity returns
  • If p-value < 0.05: oil has predictive power for equity markets
In [227]:
print('=== Granger causality: y1 (World) ===')
print('Does Oil Granger-cause MSCI World?')
gc1_world = var1_fit.test_causality('MSCI World', ['Oil'], kind='f')
print(gc1_world.summary())

print('\nDoes MSCI World Granger-cause Oil?')
gc1_oil = var1_fit.test_causality('Oil', ['MSCI World'], kind='f')
print(gc1_oil.summary())

=== Granger causality: y1 (World) ===
Does Oil Granger-cause MSCI World?
Granger causality F-test. H_0: Oil does not Granger-cause MSCI World. Conclusion: reject H_0 at 5% significance level.
================================================
Test statistic Critical value p-value     df    
------------------------------------------------
         4.904          3.842   0.027 (1, 28311)
------------------------------------------------

Does MSCI World Granger-cause Oil?
Granger causality F-test. H_0: MSCI World does not Granger-cause Oil. Conclusion: fail to reject H_0 at 5% significance level.
================================================
Test statistic Critical value p-value     df    
------------------------------------------------
         1.575          3.842   0.209 (1, 28311)
------------------------------------------------
In [228]:
print('=== Granger causality: y2 (EM) ===')
print('Does Oil Granger-cause MSCI EM?')
gc2_em = var2_fit.test_causality('MSCI EM', ['Oil'], kind='f')
print(gc2_em.summary())

print('\nDoes MSCI EM Granger-cause Oil?')
gc2_oil = var2_fit.test_causality('Oil', ['MSCI EM'], kind='f')
print(gc2_oil.summary())

=== Granger causality: y2 (EM) ===
Does Oil Granger-cause MSCI EM?

================================================
