------------------------------------------------
         5.953          3.842   0.015 (1, 28311)
------------------------------------------------

Does MSCI EM Granger-cause Oil?
Granger causality F-test. H_0: MSCI EM does not Granger-cause Oil. Conclusion: fail to reject H_0 at 5% significance level.
================================================
Test statistic Critical value p-value     df    
------------------------------------------------
         3.756          3.842   0.053 (1, 28311)
------------------------------------------------

5.5 Diagnostics — stability and residual autocorrelation¶

In [229]:
def var_diagnostics(var_fit, label):
    """
    Two diagnostics:
    1. Stability: all roots have modulus > 1
    2. Residual Ljung-Box test at lag 10
    """
    print(f'\n=== Diagnostics: {label} ===')

    # Stability
    roots = var_fit.roots
    is_stable = np.all(np.abs(roots) > 1)
    print(f'  Stable VAR (all |roots| > 1): {is_stable}')
    print(f'  Min |root|: {np.abs(roots).min():.4f}')

    # Ljung-Box on residuals
    print(f'  Ljung-Box test (lag 10) on residuals:')
    for col in var_fit.resid.columns:
        lb = acorr_ljungbox(var_fit.resid[col].dropna(), lags=[10], return_df=True)
        pval = lb['lb_pvalue'].values[0]
        print(f'    {col:15s}: p-value = {pval:.4f}  {"(autocorr. remains)" if pval < 0.05 else "(OK)"}')

var_diagnostics(var1_fit, 'y1 — World')
var_diagnostics(var2_fit, 'y2 — EM')

=== Diagnostics: y1 — World ===
  Stable VAR (all |roots| > 1): True
  Min |root|: 7.9089
  Ljung-Box test (lag 10) on residuals:
    Oil            : p-value = 0.0234  (autocorr. remains)
    Fed Rate       : p-value = 0.0004  (autocorr. remains)
    MSCI World     : p-value = 0.0000  (autocorr. remains)

=== Diagnostics: y2 — EM ===
  Stable VAR (all |roots| > 1): True
  Min |root|: 4.8318
  Ljung-Box test (lag 10) on residuals:
    Oil            : p-value = 0.0170  (autocorr. remains)
    Fed Rate       : p-value = 0.0007  (autocorr. remains)
    MSCI EM        : p-value = 0.0000  (autocorr. remains)

5.6 Impulse Response Functions (IRF)¶

We study the effect of a one standard deviation oil price shock on MSCI World and MSCI EM.

Cholesky identification¶

The reduced-form shocks $u_t$ are correlated. To identify structural shocks, we use the Cholesky decomposition:

$$\Sigma_u = PP'$$

where $P$ is a lower-triangular matrix. We then define orthogonal shocks $\varepsilon_t$ such that $u_t = P\varepsilon_t$.

Because Oil is ordered first, the oil shock $\varepsilon_1$ is identified as the first orthogonalized innovation — it is the part of the oil residual that is orthogonal to nothing (since it comes first). Economically: we assume oil shocks are exogenous in the contemporaneous period.

Confidence bands are computed by Monte Carlo simulation (300 replications) at the 95% level.

In [230]:
def plot_irf(var_fit, impulse, responses, horizon=20, repl=300, label='', tag=''):
    """
    Plot orthogonalized IRFs with Monte Carlo confidence bands.

    Parameters
    ----------
    var_fit   : fitted VAR from statsmodels
    impulse   : name of the shock variable (e.g. 'Oil')
    responses : list of response variable names
    horizon   : number of periods ahead
    repl      : Monte Carlo replications for confidence bands
    """
    irf = var_fit.irf(horizon)
    lower, upper = irf.errband_mc(orth=True, repl=repl, signif=0.05, seed=42)

    variables = list(var_fit.names)
    impulse_idx = variables.index(impulse)
    horizons = np.arange(horizon + 1)

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, len(responses), figsize=(6 * len(responses), 4), sharey=False)
    if len(responses) == 1:
        axes = [axes]

    for ax, resp in zip(axes, responses):
        resp_idx = variables.index(resp)
        irf_vals = irf.orth_irfs[:, resp_idx, impulse_idx]
        lo_vals  = lower[:, resp_idx, impulse_idx]
        hi_vals  = upper[:, resp_idx, impulse_idx]

        ax.plot(horizons, irf_vals, color='steelblue', linewidth=1.5,
                label='IRF')
        ax.fill_between(horizons, lo_vals, hi_vals, alpha=0.2,
                         color='steelblue', label='95% MC band')
        ax.axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8, linestyle='--')
        ax.set_title(f'Response of {resp}\nto 1 s.d. {impulse} shock', fontsize=10)
        ax.set_xlabel('Periods after shock')
        ax.set_ylabel('Response')
        ax.legend(fontsize=8)

    plt.suptitle(f'Orthogonalized IRF — {label}', fontsize=11)
    plt.tight_layout()
    fname = os.path.join(PLOTS_DIR, f'irf_{tag}.png')
    plt.savefig(fname, bbox_inches='tight')
    plt.show()
    print(f'  → saved: {fname}')

print('IRF function ready.')

IRF function ready.
In [231]:
# IRF: y1 — response of World and Fed Rate to oil shock
plot_irf(var1_fit, impulse='Oil',
         responses=['MSCI World', 'Fed Rate'],
         horizon=20, repl=300,
         label='y1 — World',
         tag='var1_oil_shock')

  → saved: plots\irf_var1_oil_shock.png
In [232]:
# IRF: y2 — response of EM and Fed Rate to oil shock
plot_irf(var2_fit, impulse='Oil',
         responses=['MSCI EM', 'Fed Rate'],
         horizon=20, repl=300,
         label='y2 — EM',
         tag='var2_oil_shock')

  → saved: plots\irf_var2_oil_shock.png

Section 6 — VAR: Monthly Macroeconomic Extension¶

6.1 Variable vector¶

As specified in the project plan:

$$y_3 = \begin{pmatrix} Oil_t \\ Manufacturing\ ISM_t \\ CFNAI_t \end{pmatrix}$$

This system asks: does oil predict macro activity, and how does macro activity transmit oil shocks?

In [233]:
# y3: Oil (monthly), ISM, CFNAI
var3_data = df_monthly[['roil_monthly', 'ism', 'cfnai']].dropna()
var3_data.columns = ['Oil', 'ISM', 'CFNAI']

print('VAR y3 (Monthly macro):', var3_data.shape)
print(var3_data.index[0].date(), 'to', var3_data.index[-1].date())
print()
display(var3_data.describe().round(4))

6.6 IRF — monthly macro¶

In [238]:
# Response of ISM and CFNAI to oil shock (monthly, 24 months horizon)
plot_irf(var3_fit, impulse='Oil',
         responses=['ISM', 'CFNAI'],
         horizon=24, repl=300,
         label='y3 — Monthly macro',
         tag='var3_oil_shock_macro')

  → saved: plots\irf_var3_oil_shock_macro.png

Section 7 — Forecasting¶

7.1 Setup¶

We follow the train/test split from the project plan:

  • Train: 1990 – 2015 (estimation window)
  • Test: 2016 – end of sample (out-of-sample prediction)

We estimate the VAR on the training sample only, then forecast the test period and evaluate with RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error):

$$RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{H} \sum_{h=1}^{H} (y_{T+h} - \hat{y}_{T+h})^2}$$

(train on 2021, test on 2022 — we apply the same logic on our longer sample).

In [239]:
TRAIN_END = '2015-12-31'
TEST_START = '2016-01-01'

# Daily VAR (y1: World)
train_d = var1_data.loc[:TRAIN_END]
test_d  = var1_data.loc[TEST_START:]

print(f'Daily train: {train_d.index[0].date()} to {train_d.index[-1].date()} ({len(train_d)} obs)')
print(f'Daily test : {test_d.index[0].date()}  to {test_d.index[-1].date()}  ({len(test_d)} obs)')
print()

# Monthly VAR (y3: macro)
train_m = var3_data.loc[:TRAIN_END]
test_m  = var3_data.loc[TEST_START:]

print(f'Monthly train: {train_m.index[0].date()} to {train_m.index[-1].date()} ({len(train_m)} obs)')
print(f'Monthly test : {test_m.index[0].date()}  to {test_m.index[-1].date()}  ({len(test_m)} obs)')

Daily train: 1990-01-02 to 2015-12-31 (6783 obs)
Daily test : 2016-01-01  to 2026-03-11  (2659 obs)

Monthly train: 1990-01-31 to 2015-12-31 (312 obs)
Monthly test : 2016-01-31  to 2026-02-28  (122 obs)

7.2 Daily VAR forecast (y1 — World)¶

In [240]:
# Re-estimate y1 on training sample only
var1_train = VAR(train_d).fit(p1)

# Multi-step out-of-sample forecast using the last p1 observations from the training sample
forecast_vals1 = var1_train.forecast(train_d.values[-p1:], steps=len(test_d))
forecast_df1   = pd.DataFrame(forecast_vals1, index=test_d.index, columns=var1_data.columns)

# RMSE per variable
errors1 = test_d - forecast_df1
rmse1   = pd.DataFrame(np.sqrt((errors1 ** 2).mean()), columns=['RMSE'])

display(rmse1.round(4))
rmse1.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'forecast_rmse_daily_world.csv'))
save_table(rmse1.round(4), 'forecast_rmse_daily_world.png', 'RMSE — Daily VAR y1 (World)')
RMSE
Oil 0.0236
Fed Rate 0.0532
MSCI World 0.0095 
  → saved: tables\forecast_rmse_daily_world.png
In [241]:
# Plot: forecast vs realized for MSCI World (monthly aggregation for readability)
forecast_m_world = forecast_df1['MSCI World'].resample('ME').sum()
actual_m_world   = test_d['MSCI World'].resample('ME').sum()
train_m_world    = train_d['MSCI World'].resample('ME').sum()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(13, 4))
train_m_world.loc['2013':].plot(ax=ax, color='gray', linewidth=1, label='History (train)')
actual_m_world.plot(ax=ax, color='steelblue', linewidth=1, label='Realized (test)')
forecast_m_world.plot(ax=ax, color='crimson', linewidth=1, linestyle='--',
                      label='VAR forecast')
ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp(TRAIN_END), color='black', linewidth=1, linestyle=':')
ax.set_title('Forecast vs realized — MSCI World monthly returns (2016 onwards)', fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel('Monthly log-return (sum)')
ax.legend(fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(PLOTS_DIR, 'forecast_world_vs_realized.png'), bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print('  → saved: plots/forecast_world_vs_realized.png')

  → saved: plots/forecast_world_vs_realized.png

7.3 Monthly VAR forecast (y3 — macro)¶

In [242]:
var3_train = VAR(train_m).fit(p3)

forecast_vals3 = var3_train.forecast(train_m.values[-p3:], steps=len(test_m))
forecast_df3   = pd.DataFrame(forecast_vals3, index=test_m.index, columns=var3_data.columns)

errors3 = test_m - forecast_df3
rmse3   = pd.DataFrame(np.sqrt((errors3 ** 2).mean()), columns=['RMSE'])

display(rmse3.round(4))
rmse3.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'forecast_rmse_monthly_macro.csv'))
save_table(rmse3.round(4), 'forecast_rmse_monthly_macro.png', 'RMSE — Monthly VAR y3 (Macro)')
RMSE
Oil 0.1019
ISM 5.2502
CFNAI 1.9126 
  → saved: tables\forecast_rmse_monthly_macro.png
In [243]:
# Plot: forecast vs realized for CFNAI and ISM
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(13, 7), sharex=True)

for ax, var, color in zip(axes, ['CFNAI', 'ISM'], ['navy', 'purple']):
    train_m[var].loc['2013':].plot(ax=ax, color='gray', linewidth=1, label='History (train)')
    test_m[var].plot(ax=ax, color=color, linewidth=1, label='Realized (test)')
    forecast_df3[var].plot(ax=ax, color='crimson', linewidth=1, linestyle='--', label='VAR forecast')
    ax.axvline(pd.Timestamp(TRAIN_END), color='black', linewidth=1, linestyle=':')
    ax.set_ylabel(var)
    ax.legend(fontsize=8)

axes[0].set_title('Forecast vs realized — Monthly macro VAR (2016 onwards)', fontsize=11)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(PLOTS_DIR, 'forecast_macro_vs_realized.png'), bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
print('  → saved: plots/forecast_macro_vs_realized.png')

  → saved: plots/forecast_macro_vs_realized.png

7.4 RMSE comparison summary¶

In [244]:
# Consolidate RMSE results
rmse_all = pd.concat([rmse1.rename(columns={'RMSE':'RMSE_daily_y1'}),
                      rmse3.rename(columns={'RMSE':'RMSE_monthly_y3'})], axis=0)

rmse_all.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'forecast_rmse_all.csv'))

print('RMSE summary:')
display(rmse_all.round(5))

print()
# Daily financial VAR
std_world = var1_data['MSCI World'].std()
rmse_world = rmse1.loc['MSCI World', 'RMSE']
print(f"MSCI World daily std = {std_world:.4f}")
print(f"MSCI World daily RMSE = {rmse_world:.4f}")
print(f"RMSE/std ratio = {rmse_world / std_world:.2f}")

# Monthly macro VAR
std_ism = var3_data['ISM'].std()
std_cfnai = var3_data['CFNAI'].std()
rmse_ism = rmse3.loc['ISM', 'RMSE']
rmse_cfnai = rmse3.loc['CFNAI', 'RMSE']

print()
print(f"ISM monthly std = {std_ism:.4f}, RMSE = {rmse_ism:.4f}, ratio = {rmse_ism / std_ism:.2f}")
print(f"CFNAI monthly std = {std_cfnai:.4f}, RMSE = {rmse_cfnai:.4f}, ratio = {rmse_cfnai / std_cfnai:.2f}")

8. Files saved to tables/ and plots/¶

File Content
var_daily_summary_stats.csv Summary stats for VAR variables
var1_lag_selection.csv/png Lag selection y1 (World)
var2_lag_selection.csv/png Lag selection y2 (EM)
var3_lag_selection.csv/png Lag selection y3 (Monthly macro)
irf_var1_oil_shock.png IRF: oil shock → World + Fed Rate
irf_var2_oil_shock.png IRF: oil shock → EM + Fed Rate
irf_var3_oil_shock_macro.png IRF: oil shock → ISM + CFNAI
forecast_rmse_daily_world.csv/png RMSE daily y1 (2016–end)
forecast_rmse_monthly_macro.csv/png RMSE monthly y3 (2016–end)
forecast_rmse_all.csv Consolidated RMSE
forecast_world_vs_realized.png Forecast plot — MSCI World
forecast_macro_vs_realized.png Forecast plot — CFNAI + ISM

Step 6 — VAR Robustness Check¶

Robustness Check 1 — Sensitivity to Lag Length¶

A key modelling choice in VAR estimation is the selection of the lag length (p). This choice involves a trade-off: a small number of lags may omit relevant dynamics (underfitting), while too many lags may introduce noise and reduce estimation efficiency (overfitting).

In the baseline specification, we used BIC for the daily VAR

To assess whether our results are sensitive to this modelling choice, we re-estimate the VAR using the lag length selected by AIC. We then compare the key outputs across specifications, including:

  • Granger causality results
  • Model diagnostics
  • Impulse Response Functions (IRFs)

This robustness check allows us to evaluate whether the main conclusions are driven by the chosen lag structure or remain stable under a less parsimonious specification.

0. Set Up¶

1. Load data¶

Section 5 — VAR: Daily Financial Variables¶

5.1 Variable vectors¶

We estimate two daily VAR systems as specified in the project plan:

$$y_1 = \begin{pmatrix} Oil_t \\ FedRate_t \\ MSCI\ World_t \end{pmatrix} \qquad y_2 = \begin{pmatrix} Oil_t \\ FedRate_t \\ MSCI\ EM_t \end{pmatrix}$$

Oil is ordered first so that its contemporaneous shock can affect the other variables in the same period. This is the standard Cholesky identification assumption for oil price shocks.

In [245]:
# y1: Oil, Fed Rate (yield changes), MSCI World
var1_data = df_daily[['roil', 'rrate', 'rworld']].dropna()
var1_data.columns = ['Oil', 'Fed Rate', 'MSCI World']

# y2: Oil, Fed Rate, MSCI EM
var2_data = df_daily[['roil', 'rrate', 'rem']].dropna()
var2_data.columns = ['Oil', 'Fed Rate', 'MSCI EM']

print('VAR y1 (World):', var1_data.shape, '|', var1_data.index[0].date(), 'to', var1_data.index[-1].date())
print('VAR y2 (EM):   ', var2_data.shape)
print()

# Summary statistics
stats = var1_data.describe().T[['mean','std','min','max']].round(4)
display(stats)
stats.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'var_daily_summary_stats.csv'))

VAR y1 (World): (9442, 3) | 1990-01-02 to 2026-03-11
VAR y2 (EM):    (9442, 3)
mean std min max
Oil 0.0002 0.0219 -0.3856 0.1908
Fed Rate -0.0004 0.0583 -0.4736 0.3602
MSCI World 0.0002 0.0094 -0.1044 0.0910

5.2 Lag selection¶

We test lag lengths from 1 to 10 days using AIC, BIC, FPE, and HQIC information criteria.

  • AIC tends to select more lags (lower penalty)
  • BIC is more parsimonious (higher penalty for additional parameters)
In [324]:
print('=== Lag selection: y1 (World) ===')
model1 = VAR(var1_data)
lag_sel1 = model1.select_order(10)

lag_table1 = pd.DataFrame({
    'AIC':  lag_sel1.ics['aic'],
    'BIC':  lag_sel1.ics['bic'],
    'HQIC': lag_sel1.ics['hqic'],
    'FPE':  lag_sel1.ics['fpe'],
})
lag_table1.index.name = 'Lag'

lag_display = lag_table1.copy()
lag_display['FPE'] = lag_table1['FPE'].apply(lambda x: f'{x:.3e}')

print(lag_sel1.summary())
display(lag_display)
lag_table1.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'var1_lag_selection.csv'))
save_table(lag_display, 'var1_lag_selection.png', 'Lag selection — y1 (World)')

=== Lag selection: y1 (World) ===
 VAR Order Selection (* highlights the minimums)

10      -22.77      -22.70   1.287e-10      -22.75

In [323]:
print('=== Lag selection: y2 (EM) ===')
model2 = VAR(var2_data)
lag_sel2 = model2.select_order(10)

lag_table2 = pd.DataFrame({
    'AIC':  lag_sel2.ics['aic'],
    'BIC':  lag_sel2.ics['bic'],
    'HQIC': lag_sel2.ics['hqic'],
    'FPE':  lag_sel2.ics['fpe'],
})
lag_table2.index.name = 'Lag'

lag_display2 = lag_table2.copy()
lag_display2['FPE'] = lag_table2['FPE'].apply(lambda x: f'{x:.3e}')

print(lag_sel2.summary())
display(lag_display2)
lag_table2.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'var2_lag_selection.csv'))
save_table(lag_display2, 'var2_lag_selection.png', 'Lag selection — y2 (EM)')

5.3 Estimation¶

Using AIC

In [248]:
p1_rb = max(1, lag_sel1.aic)
p2_rb = max(1, lag_sel2.aic)

print(f'Selected lags: y1 (World) = {p1_rb}, y2 (EM) = {p2_rb}')

var1_fit_rb = model1.fit(p1_rb)
var2_fit_rb = model2.fit(p2_rb)

print(var1_fit_rb.summary())
print(var2_fit_rb.summary())

Selected lags: y1 (World) = 7, y2 (EM) = 7

5.4 Granger causality tests¶

Granger causality tests whether past values of oil returns provide statistically significant information to predict current equity returns, beyond what is already in the equity returns' own past.

  • H0: Oil does NOT Granger-cause equity returns
  • If p-value < 0.05: oil has predictive power for equity markets
In [249]:
print('=== Granger causality: y1 (World) ===')
print('Does Oil Granger-cause MSCI World?')
gc1_world = var1_fit_rb.test_causality('MSCI World', ['Oil'], kind='f')
print(gc1_world.summary())

print('\nDoes MSCI World Granger-cause Oil?')
gc1_oil = var1_fit_rb.test_causality('Oil', ['MSCI World'], kind='f')
print(gc1_oil.summary())

=== Granger causality: y1 (World) ===
Does Oil Granger-cause MSCI World?
Granger causality F-test. H_0: Oil does not Granger-cause MSCI World. Conclusion: reject H_0 at 5% significance level.
==========================================================
Test statistic Critical value p-value          df         
----------------------------------------------------------
         3.977          2.010   0.000 (7, np.int64(28239))
----------------------------------------------------------

Does MSCI World Granger-cause Oil?
Granger causality F-test. H_0: MSCI World does not Granger-cause Oil. Conclusion: reject H_0 at 5% significance level.
==========================================================
Test statistic Critical value p-value          df         
----------------------------------------------------------
         3.277          2.010   0.002 (7, np.int64(28239))
----------------------------------------------------------
In [250]:
print('=== Granger causality: y2 (EM) ===')
print('Does Oil Granger-cause MSCI EM?')
gc2_em = var2_fit_rb.test_causality('MSCI EM', ['Oil'], kind='f')
print(gc2_em.summary())

print('\nDoes MSCI EM Granger-cause Oil?')
gc2_oil = var2_fit_rb.test_causality('Oil', ['MSCI EM'], kind='f')
print(gc2_oil.summary())

=== Granger causality: y2 (EM) ===
Does Oil Granger-cause MSCI EM?
Granger causality F-test. H_0: Oil does not Granger-cause MSCI EM. Conclusion: reject H_0 at 5% significance level.
==========================================================
Test statistic Critical value p-value          df         
----------------------------------------------------------
         2.886          2.010   0.005 (7, np.int64(28239))
----------------------------------------------------------

Does MSCI EM Granger-cause Oil?
Granger causality F-test. H_0: MSCI EM does not Granger-cause Oil. Conclusion: reject H_0 at 5% significance level.
==========================================================
Test statistic Critical value p-value          df         
----------------------------------------------------------
         2.147          2.010   0.036 (7, np.int64(28239))
----------------------------------------------------------

5.5 Diagnostics — stability and residual autocorrelation¶

5.6 Impulse Response Functions (IRF)¶

We study the effect of a one standard deviation oil price shock on MSCI World and MSCI EM.

Cholesky identification¶

The reduced-form shocks $u_t$ are correlated. To identify structural shocks, we use the Cholesky decomposition:

$$\Sigma_u = PP'$$

where $P$ is a lower-triangular matrix. We then define orthogonal shocks $\varepsilon_t$ such that $u_t = P\varepsilon_t$.

Because Oil is ordered first, the oil shock $\varepsilon_1$ is identified as the first orthogonalized innovation — it is the part of the oil residual that is orthogonal to nothing (since it comes first). Economically: we assume oil shocks are exogenous in the contemporaneous period.

Confidence bands are computed by Monte Carlo simulation (300 replications) at the 95% level.

In [251]:
# IRF: y1 — response of World and Fed Rate to oil shock
plot_irf(var1_fit_rb, impulse='Oil',
         responses=['MSCI World', 'Fed Rate'],
         horizon=20, repl=300,
         label='y1 — World',
         tag='var1_rb_oil_shock')

  → saved: plots\irf_var1_rb_oil_shock.png
In [252]:
# IRF: y2 — response of EM and Fed Rate to oil shock
plot_irf(var2_fit_rb, impulse='Oil',
         responses=['MSCI EM', 'Fed Rate'],
         horizon=20, repl=300,
         label='y2 — EM',
         tag='var2_rb_oil_shock')

  → saved: plots\irf_var2_rb_oil_shock.png

Section 6 — VAR: Monthly Macroeconomic Extension¶

6.1 Variable vector¶

As specified in the project plan:

$$y_3 = \begin{pmatrix} Oil_t \\ Manufacturing\ ISM_t \\ CFNAI_t \end{pmatrix}$$

This system asks: does oil predict macro activity, and how does macro activity transmit oil shocks?

In [253]:
# y3: Oil (monthly), ISM, CFNAI
var3_data = df_monthly[['roil_monthly', 'ism', 'cfnai']].dropna()
var3_data.columns = ['Oil', 'ISM', 'CFNAI']

print('VAR y3 (Monthly macro):', var3_data.shape)
print(var3_data.index[0].date(), 'to', var3_data.index[-1].date())
print()
display(var3_data.describe().round(4))

VAR y3 (Monthly macro): (434, 3)
1990-01-31 to 2026-02-28
Oil ISM CFNAI
count 434.0000 434.0000 434.0000
mean 0.0030 52.2048 -0.0733
std 0.0926 4.8842 1.1138
min -0.6339 34.5000 -18.2600
25% -0.0461 49.0000 -0.2875
50% 0.0077 52.5500 0.0100
75% 0.0607 55.7000 0.2400
max 0.3660 63.8000 6.3000

6.2 Lag selection¶

In [254]:
model3 = VAR(var3_data)
lag_sel3 = model3.select_order(12)

lag_table3 = pd.DataFrame({
    'AIC':  lag_sel3.ics['aic'],
    'BIC':  lag_sel3.ics['bic'],
    'HQIC': lag_sel3.ics['hqic'],
    'FPE':  lag_sel3.ics['fpe'],
})
lag_table3.index.name = 'Lag'

print(lag_sel3.summary())
display(lag_table3.round(4))
lag_table3.to_csv(os.path.join(TABLES_DIR, 'var3_lag_selection.csv'))
save_table(lag_table3.round(4), 'var3_lag_selection.png', 'Lag selection — y3 (Monthly macro)')

6.3 Estimation¶

In [255]:
p3_rb = max(1, lag_sel3.aic)
print(f'Selected lag (AIC): {p3_rb}')

var3_fit_rb = model3.fit(p3_rb)
print(var3_fit_rb.summary())

6.4 Granger causality¶

In [256]:
print('=== Granger causality: y3_rb (Monthly macro) ===')
print('Does Oil Granger-cause ISM?')
print(var3_fit_rb.test_causality('ISM', ['Oil'], kind='f').summary())

print('\nDoes Oil Granger-cause CFNAI?')
print(var3_fit_rb.test_causality('CFNAI', ['Oil'], kind='f').summary())

print('\nDoes ISM Granger-cause CFNAI?')
print(var3_fit_rb.test_causality('CFNAI', ['ISM'], kind='f').summary())

=== Granger causality: y3_rb (Monthly macro) ===
Does Oil Granger-cause ISM?
Granger causality F-test. H_0: Oil does not Granger-cause ISM. Conclusion: reject H_0 at 5% significance level.
=========================================================

---------------------------------------------------------
         11.25          3.003   0.000 (2, np.int64(1275))
---------------------------------------------------------

---------------------------------------------------------

Does ISM Granger-cause CFNAI?

         10.36          3.003   0.000 (2, np.int64(1275))

In [257]:
var_diagnostics(var3_fit_rb, 'y3_rb — Monthly macro')

=== Diagnostics: y3_rb — Monthly macro ===

  Min |root|: 1.0848

    Oil            : p-value = 0.2238  (OK)
    ISM            : p-value = 0.2892  (OK)
    CFNAI          : p-value = 0.4438  (OK)

6.6 IRF — monthly macro¶

In [258]:
# Response of ISM and CFNAI to oil shock (monthly, 24 months horizon)
plot_irf(var3_fit_rb, impulse='Oil',
         responses=['ISM', 'CFNAI'],
         horizon=24, repl=300,
         label='y3 — Monthly macro',
         tag='var3_rb_oil_shock_macro')

  → saved: plots\irf_var3_rb_oil_shock_macro.png
